This plan outlines the key components, technologies, data flows, and development phases necessary to build a robust, real-time drafting assistant for Magic: The Gathering (MTG). --- ## 1. Overview Draft Navigator Overlay App is an intelligent, real-time drafting assistant designed to enhance the player's decision-making process during MTG drafts. It provides targeted recommendations by analyzing card choices based on win rates, synergy, archetype flexibility, and current meta trends. The app overlays seamlessly onto the draft screen, offering non-intrusive tooltips, synergy bars, and color-coded visual cues to aid players in building powerful and cohesive decks. --- ## 2. Architectural Diagram (Conceptual Overview) Since a visual diagram can't be provided here, below is a textual representation of the architecture: - Frontend (Client-Side) - User Interface (Overlay) - Real-Time Updates - User Interaction Handling - Backend (Server-Side) - API Integrations (17lands, Scryfall) - Data Processing and Storage - Recommendation Engine - Data Sources - 17lands API - Scryfall API - MTG Arena Logs - Communication - WebSockets for Real-Time Data - RESTful APIs for Data Fetching --- ## 3. Technology Stack ### Frontend: - Framework: React.js or Vue.js - State Management: Redux (for React) or Vuex (for Vue) - Styling: CSS3, SASS/SCSS, Tailwind CSS or Bootstrap - Real-Time Communication: WebSockets (e.g., Socket.IO) - Overlay Integration: Electron.js (if desktop) or Browser Extensions (if web-based) ### Backend: - Framework: Node.js with Express.js or Python with Django/Flask - Real-Time Communication: WebSockets (e.g., Socket.IO for Node.js) - Database: PostgreSQL or MongoDB - Caching: Redis - API Integration: RESTful APIs for 17lands and Scryfall - Hosting: AWS, Google Cloud Platform, or Azure ### Data Processing & Analytics: - Languages: Python (for data analysis), JavaScript - Libraries: Pandas, NumPy, Scikit-learn for machine learning (if applicable) - Message Queues: RabbitMQ or Kafka (for handling data streams) ### Other Tools: - Version Control: Git with GitHub or GitLab - CI/CD: Jenkins, Travis CI, or GitHub Actions - Testing: Jest, Mocha (for frontend), PyTest or similar for backend - Containerization: Docker - Orchestration: Kubernetes (for scaling) --- ## 4. Data Sources and Integration ### a. 17lands API: - Purpose: Fetch win rates, ALSA (Average Last Seen At), and trophy deck statistics. - Integration: - Use RESTful API endpoints to retrieve real-time meta data. - Implement periodic polling or webhook listeners if supported for updates. ### b. Scryfall API: - Purpose: Access comprehensive card data, including card images, mana costs, mechanics, and other metadata. - Integration: - Utilize RESTful API to fetch card details. - Implement caching strategies to minimize API calls and reduce latency. ### c. MTG Arena Logs: - Purpose: Capture real-time draft information and player actions. - Integration: - Develop or utilize existing log parsers to extract relevant data from MTG Arena. - Ensure compliance with MTG Arena’s terms of service regarding data access. --- ## 5. Backend Components ### a. API Integration Layer: - Functionality: - Interface with 17lands and Scryfall APIs. - Handle authentication, rate limiting, and data fetching. - Implementation: - Create modular services for each external API. - Implement retry mechanisms and error handling. ### b. Data Processing Module: - Functionality: - Process and analyze fetched data to compute synergy scores, power levels, and other metrics. - Update recommendations in real-time based on draft progression. - Implementation: - Utilize Python for data analysis tasks. - Implement efficient algorithms for real-time calculations. ### c. Recommendation Engine: - Functionality: - Generate top 2-4 card recommendations per pick based on multiple factors. - Balance between raw power and synergy considerations. - Implementation: - Develop scoring algorithms that weigh different factors (win rates, synergy, archetype flexibility). - Use machine learning models if necessary for predictive analytics. ### d. Database Management: - Functionality: - Store user data, draft histories, deck compositions, and cached API data. - Ensure data integrity and quick retrieval. - Implementation: - Use relational databases (PostgreSQL) for structured data. - Utilize NoSQL databases (MongoDB) for flexible data storage if needed. ### e. Real-Time Communication Server: - Functionality: - Facilitate real-time data transmission between frontend and backend. - Manage WebSocket connections for live updates. - Implementation: - Use Socket.IO for handling WebSockets in Node.js. - Ensure scalability to handle multiple concurrent connections. --- ## 6. Frontend Components ### a. User Interface (Overlay): - Functionality: - Display real-time recommendations, synergy scores, mana curve visualizer, pivot alerts, and other key metrics. - Ensure seamless integration without obstructing the draft view. - Implementation: - Develop as a browser extension or an overlay application using Electron.js. - Use React or Vue components for modularity and reusability. ### b. Real-Time Updates: - Functionality: - Reflect changes as new cards are drafted, updating recommendations and visual indicators instantly. - Implementation: - Utilize WebSockets to receive live data from the backend. - Implement state management to handle dynamic UI updates. ### c. Visual Elements: - Components: - Synergy Bars: Visual representation of synergy scores. - Mana Curve Visualizer: Graphical display of the deck’s mana curve. - Color-Coded Cues: Indicators for power vs. synergy, pivot alerts, etc. - Tooltips: Hoverable elements providing detailed card metrics. - Implementation: - Use D3.js or Chart.js for creating interactive visualizations. - Ensure responsiveness and accessibility across different screen sizes. ### d. User Interaction Handling: - Functionality: - Allow users to interact with recommendations, such as selecting a card, viewing detailed stats, or customizing settings. - Implementation: - Implement interactive components with clear affordances. - Ensure minimal latency in user interactions to maintain a smooth drafting experience. --- ## 7. Real-Time Data Processing ### a. Data Streaming: - Functionality: - Stream draft data from MTG Arena to the backend in real-time. - Process incoming data to update recommendations instantly. - Implementation: - Use WebSockets for continuous data streaming. - Implement efficient data parsing and processing pipelines. ### b. Data Synchronization: - Functionality: - Ensure consistency between frontend and backend data states. - Implementation: - Use state management libraries (Redux/Vuex) to synchronize data. - Implement conflict resolution strategies to handle discrepancies. ### c. Performance Optimization: - Functionality: - Maintain low latency for real-time updates to ensure a seamless user experience. - Implementation: - Optimize algorithms for speed and efficiency. - Use caching (Redis) to store frequently accessed data. - Implement load balancing and horizontal scaling on the backend. --- ## 8. Algorithms and Analysis ### a. Synergy Scoring Algorithm: - Inputs: Drafted cards, card mechanics, archetype data. - Process: - Identify card interactions, combos, and thematic connections. - Assign synergy scores based on predefined rules or machine learning models. - Output: Synergy score for each card relative to the current deck. ### b. Power Level Calculation: - Inputs: Card win rates, ALSA, GIH WR. - Process: - Aggregate win rates and other performance metrics. - Normalize scores to compare across different cards and formats. - Output: Power score for each card. ### c. Archetype Detection and Flexibility: - Inputs: Drafted cards, archetype definitions. - Process: - Match drafted cards against known archetypes. - Calculate flexibility based on how well cards fit into multiple archetypes. - Output: Suggested archetypes and flexibility scores. ### d. Mana Curve Analysis: - Inputs: Mana costs of drafted cards. - Process: - Categorize cards by mana cost. - Compare against optimal mana curve distributions. - Output: Mana curve balance indicators and suggestions for curve adjustments. ### e. Pivot Detection Logic: - Inputs: Passed card signals, current deck composition, meta trends. - Process: - Analyze signals from passed packs to identify underdrafted colors or archetypes. - Determine optimal pivot points based on available data. - Output: Pivot alerts and recommendations. ### f. Meta-Aware Recommendation System: - Inputs: Current meta data, win rates, trophy deck statistics. - Process: - Continuously update meta trends based on incoming data. - Adjust card recommendations to align with the evolving meta. - Output: Meta-relevant draft suggestions. --- ## 9. UI/UX Design ### a. User Interface Layout: - Overlay Placement: Positioned to not obstruct the main draft view, possibly at the bottom or side of the screen. - Visual Hierarchy: Prioritize key information (e.g., top recommendations) while keeping secondary data accessible but unobtrusive. - Color Coding: Use distinct colors for power vs. synergy indicators, pivot alerts, and balance warnings. ### b. User Experience Considerations: - Non-Intrusiveness: Ensure the overlay does not interfere with the draft flow or visibility of card details. - Responsiveness: Maintain smooth performance with real-time updates without lag. - Customization Options: Allow users to adjust the level of detail, toggle features on/off, and customize visual elements to their preference. ### c. Accessibility: - Color Contrast: Ensure sufficient contrast for readability. - Tooltips and Annotations: Provide clear and concise information without overwhelming the user. - Keyboard Navigation: Support for users who prefer keyboard interactions. --- ## 10. API Integrations #### 1.1 Integrate 17lands API for Win Rates and ALSA Data Prompt: > "Write a Node.js service using Express.js that connects to the 17lands API to fetch real-time win rates and ALSA (Average Last Seen At) data for MTG cards. Ensure the service handles API authentication, rate limiting, and error handling." Implementation Steps: 1. Set Up the Node.js Project: - Initialize the Project: mkdir draft-navigator-backend cd draft-navigator-backend npm init -y - Install Necessary Dependencies: npm install express axios dotenv express-rate-limit winston - express : Web framework for Node.js. - axios : Promise-based HTTP client. - dotenv : Loads environment variables from a .env file. - express-rate-limit : Middleware to limit repeated requests. - winston : Logging library. 2. Configure Environment Variables: - Create a .env File in the Project Root: PORT=3000 SEVENTEENLANDS_API_KEY=your_17lands_api_key SEVENTEENLANDS_API_URL=https://api.17lands.com/v1 RATE_LIMIT_WINDOW_MS=60000 RATE_LIMIT_MAX=60 > Note: Replace your_17lands_api_key with your actual API key from 17lands. 3. Create the Express Server: - Create an index.js File: // index.js require('dotenv').config(); const express = require('express'); const axios = require('axios'); const rateLimit = require('express-rate-limit'); const winston = require('winston'); const app = express(); const PORT = process.env.PORT || 3000; // Configure Winston Logger const logger = winston.createLogger({ level: 'info', format: winston.format.combine( winston.format.timestamp(), winston.format.json() ), transports: [ new winston.transports.Console(), new winston.transports.File({ filename: 'error.log', level: 'error' }), new winston.transports.File({ filename: 'combined.log' }), ], }); // Rate Limiting Middleware const limiter = rateLimit({ windowMs: parseInt(process.env.RATE_LIMIT_WINDOW_MS) || 60000, // 1 minute max: parseInt(process.env.RATE_LIMIT_MAX) || 60, // limit each IP to 60 requests per windowMs message: 'Too many requests from this IP, please try again after a minute.', handler: (req, res, next, options) => { logger.warn( Rate limit exceeded for IP: ${req.ip} ); res.status(options.statusCode).json({ message: options.message }); }, }); app.use(limiter); // 17lands API Service const seventeenLandsAPI = axios.create({ baseURL: process.env.SEVENTEENLANDS_API_URL, headers: { 'Authorization': Bearer ${process.env.SEVENTEENLANDS_API_KEY} , 'Content-Type': 'application/json', }, timeout: 5000, // 5 seconds timeout }); // Endpoint to Fetch Win Rates and ALSA Data for a Specific Card app.get('/api/cards/:cardName/stats', async (req, res) => { const { cardName } = req.params; try { const response = await seventeenLandsAPI.get( /cards/${encodeURIComponent(cardName)}/stats ); res.json(response.data); } catch (error) { if (error.response) { // API responded with a status outside 2xx logger.error( 17lands API Error: ${error.response.status} - ${error.response.data} ); res.status(error.response.status).json({ message: error.response.data }); } else if (error.request) { // No response received logger.error( 17lands API No Response: ${error.message} ); res.status(503).json({ message: '17lands API is unavailable. Please try again later.' }); } else { // Other errors logger.error( Error fetching 17lands data: ${error.message} ); res.status(500).json({ message: 'Internal Server Error' }); } } }); // Start the Server app.listen(PORT, () => { logger.info( 17lands API Service running on port ${PORT} ); }); 4. Explanation of the Code: - Environment Variables: - dotenv loads environment variables from the .env file to keep sensitive information secure. - Winston Logger: - Configured to log information and errors both to the console and to log files ( error.log for errors and combined.log for all logs). - Rate Limiting: - Limits each IP to 60 requests per minute using express-rate-limit . - Logs a warning if the rate limit is exceeded and sends a JSON response to the client. - 17lands API Service: - Uses axios to create a service with the base URL and necessary headers, including the API key. - Sets a timeout of 5 seconds to prevent hanging requests. - API Endpoint ( /api/cards/:cardName/stats ): - Fetches win rates and ALSA data for a specific card by its name. - Handles different error scenarios: - API Errors: Logs and returns the API's error response. - No Response: Logs the issue and informs the client that the API is unavailable. - Other Errors: Logs and returns a generic internal server error. - Starting the Server: - The server listens on the specified port and logs a confirmation message. 5. Running the Service: - Ensure Redis is Installed and Running (if used elsewhere in the project). - Start the Server: node index.js - Test the Endpoint: You can use tools like curl or Postman to test the API. curl http://localhost:3000/api/cards/Black%20Lotus/stats > Note: Replace Black%20Lotus with the URL-encoded name of the card you want to query. --- #### 1.2 Integrate Scryfall API for Comprehensive Card Data Prompt: > "Develop a Python script using the requests library to interact with the Scryfall API. Fetch detailed card information, including images, mana costs, mechanics, and metadata. Implement caching with Redis to minimize API calls and improve performance." Implementation Steps: 1. Set Up the Python Environment: - Create a Project Directory: mkdir draft-navigator-scryfall cd draft-navigator-scryfall - Create and Activate a Virtual Environment: python3 -m venv venv source venv/bin/activate - Install Necessary Dependencies: pip install requests redis python-dotenv - requests : HTTP library for Python. - redis : Python client for Redis. - python-dotenv : Loads environment variables from a .env file. 2. Configure Environment Variables: - Create a .env File in the Project Root: SCRYFALL_API_URL=https://api.scryfall.com REDIS_HOST=localhost REDIS_PORT=6379 REDIS_DB=0 REDIS_PASSWORD=your_redis_password_if_any CACHE_EXPIRATION=86400 # 24 hours in seconds > Note: If your Redis server does not require a password, you can omit the REDIS_PASSWORD variable. 3. Create the Python Script: - Create a scryfall_fetcher.py File: # scryfall_fetcher.py import os import requests import redis import json from dotenv import load_dotenv # Load environment variables load_dotenv() SCRYFALL_API_URL = os.getenv('SCRYFALL_API_URL', 'https://api.scryfall.com') REDIS_HOST = os.getenv('REDIS_HOST', 'localhost') REDIS_PORT = int(os.getenv('REDIS_PORT', 6379)) REDIS_DB = int(os.getenv('REDIS_DB', 0)) REDIS_PASSWORD = os.getenv('REDIS_PASSWORD', None) CACHE_EXPIRATION = int(os.getenv('CACHE_EXPIRATION', 86400)) # 24 hours # Initialize Redis client try: if REDIS_PASSWORD: cache = redis.StrictRedis(host=REDIS_HOST, port=REDIS_PORT, db=REDIS_DB, password=REDIS_PASSWORD, decode_responses=True) else: cache = redis.StrictRedis(host=REDIS_HOST, port=REDIS_PORT, db=REDIS_DB, decode_responses=True) # Test connection cache.ping() print("Connected to Redis successfully.") except redis.ConnectionError as e: print(f"Redis connection error: {e}") exit(1) def fetch_card_data(card_name): """ Fetch detailed card information from Scryfall API. """ # Check if data is cached cache_key = f"scryfall:card:{card_name.lower()}" cached_data = cache.get(cache_key) if cached_data: print(f"Cache hit for card: {card_name}") return json.loads(cached_data) print(f"Cache miss for card: {card_name}. Fetching from Scryfall API.") try: response = requests.get(f"{SCRYFALL_API_URL}/cards/named", params={'fuzzy': card_name}, timeout=10) response.raise_for_status() card_data = response.json() # Cache the response cache.setex(cache_key, CACHE_EXPIRATION, json.dumps(card_data)) return card_data except requests.exceptions.HTTPError as http_err: print(f"HTTP error occurred: {http_err} - {response.text}") return None except requests.exceptions.RequestException as err: print(f"Error fetching card data: {err}") return None def main(): # Example usage card_names = [ "Black Lotus", "Sol Ring", "Time Walk", "Mox Sapphire", "Lightning Bolt" ] for card in card_names: data = fetch_card_data(card) if data: print(f"Card: {data['name']}") print(f"Mana Cost: {data.get('mana_cost', 'N/A')}") print(f"Type Line: {data.get('type_line', 'N/A')}") print(f"Oracle Text: {data.get('oracle_text', 'N/A')}") print(f"Image URL: {data['image_uris']['normal'] if 'image_uris' in data else 'N/A'}") print("-" * 40) else: print(f"Failed to fetch data for card: {card}") print("-" * 40) if __name__ == "__main__": main() 4. Explanation of the Code: - Environment Variables: - Configures the Scryfall API URL and Redis connection details. - CACHE_EXPIRATION sets how long (in seconds) the card data is cached in Redis (default is 24 hours). - Redis Client Initialization: - Connects to the Redis server using the provided host, port, database number, and password if applicable. - Tests the connection with cache.ping() and exits if the connection fails. - fetch_card_data Function: - Checks if the card data is already cached in Redis using a key pattern like scryfall:card:black lotus . - If cached, returns the data directly from Redis. - If not cached, makes a GET request to the Scryfall API's /cards/named endpoint using the fuzzy parameter to handle approximate card names. - On successful retrieval, caches the data in Redis with an expiration time. - Handles HTTP errors and other request exceptions gracefully. - main Function: - Demonstrates how to fetch and display data for a list of example card names. - Prints key card details like name, mana cost, type line, oracle text, and image URL. 5. Running the Script: - Ensure Redis is Installed and Running: # On macOS using Homebrew brew install redis brew services start redis # On Ubuntu/Debian sudo apt-get install redis-server sudo systemctl enable redis-server.service sudo systemctl start redis-server.service - Run the Python Script: python scryfall_fetcher.py - Expected Output: Connected to Redis successfully. Fetching from Scryfall API. Card: Sol Ring Mana Cost: {1} Type Line: Artifact Oracle Text: {T}: Add {C}{C}. Image URL: https://c1.scryfall.com/file/scryfall-cards/normal/front/c/a/ca10d63c-a6c2-4f23-ae63-879678d6c3e5.jpg?1687522180 ---------------------------------------- Cache miss for card: Time Walk. Fetching from Scryfall API. Card: Time Walk Mana Cost: {1}{U} Type Line: Sorcery Oracle Text: Take an extra turn after this one. Fetching from Scryfall API. Card: Mox Sapphire Mana Cost: {0} Type Line: Artifact Oracle Text: {T}: Add {U}. Image URL: https://c1.scryfall.com/file/scryfall-cards/normal/front/a/c/acb0ddc5-bb8c-4cd3-9a4b-a4ab6dd4b3b4.jpg?1687522188 ---------------------------------------- Cache miss for card: Lightning Bolt. Fetching from Scryfall API. Card: Lightning Bolt Mana Cost: {R} Type Line: Instant Oracle Text: Lightning Bolt deals 3 damage to any target. Image URL: https://c1.scryfall.com/file/scryfall-cards/normal/front/9/0/90c1a6b3-cfd5-4c4b-9f93-49b6797a90b5.jpg?1687522192 ---------------------------------------- > Note: On subsequent runs, the script should hit the cache for these cards, reducing API calls and speeding up data retrieval. --- #### 1.3 Set Up WebSocket Server for Real-Time Data Streaming Prompt: > "Implement a WebSocket server using Socket.IO in Node.js to enable real-time communication between the frontend and backend. Ensure the server can handle multiple concurrent connections and efficiently broadcast updates to connected clients." Implementation Steps: 1. Extend the Existing Node.js Project: Assuming you're continuing from the previous 17lands API service, integrate Socket.IO into your index.js file. 2. Install Socket.IO on the Backend: npm install socket.io 3. Modify the index.js to Include Socket.IO: // index.js require('dotenv').config(); const express = require('express'); const axios = require('axios'); const rateLimit = require('express-rate-limit'); const winston = require('winston'); const http = require('http'); const { Server } = require('socket.io'); const app = express(); const PORT = process.env.PORT || 3000; // Configure Winston Logger const logger = winston.createLogger({ level: 'info', format: winston.format.combine( winston.format.timestamp(), winston.format.json() ), transports: [ new winston.transports.Console(), new winston.transports.File({ filename: 'error.log', level: 'error' }), new winston.transports.File({ filename: 'combined.log' }), ], }); // Rate Limiting Middleware const limiter = rateLimit({ windowMs: parseInt(process.env.RATE_LIMIT_WINDOW_MS) || 60000, // 1 minute max: parseInt(process.env.RATE_LIMIT_MAX) || 60, // limit each IP to 60 requests per windowMs message: 'Too many requests from this IP, please try again after a minute.', handler: (req, res, next, options) => { logger.warn( Rate limit exceeded for IP: ${req.ip} ); res.status(options.statusCode).json({ message: options.message }); }, }); app.use(limiter); // 17lands API Service const seventeenLandsAPI = axios.create({ baseURL: process.env.SEVENTEENLANDS_API_URL, headers: { 'Authorization': Bearer ${process.env.SEVENTEENLANDS_API_KEY} , 'Content-Type': 'application/json', }, timeout: 5000, // 5 seconds timeout }); // Endpoint to Fetch Win Rates and ALSA Data for a Specific Card app.get('/api/cards/:cardName/stats', async (req, res) => { const { cardName } = req.params; try { const response = await seventeenLandsAPI.get( /cards/${encodeURIComponent(cardName)}/stats ); res.json(response.data); } catch (error) { if (error.response) { // API responded with a status outside 2xx logger.error( 17lands API Error: ${error.response.status} - ${error.response.data} ); res.status(error.response.status).json({ message: error.response.data }); } else if (error.request) { // No response received logger.error( 17lands API No Response: ${error.message} ); res.status(503).json({ message: '17lands API is unavailable. Please try again later.' }); } else { // Other errors logger.error( Error fetching 17lands data: ${error.message} ); res.status(500).json({ message: 'Internal Server Error' }); } } }); // Create HTTP Server const server = http.createServer(app); // Initialize Socket.IO Server const io = new Server(server, { cors: { origin: '*', // Adjust this in production for security methods: ['GET', 'POST'], }, }); // Handle Socket.IO Connections io.on('connection', (socket) => { logger.info( New client connected: ${socket.id} ); // Example: Listen for a custom event from the client socket.on('joinDraft', (draftId) => { socket.join( draft_${draftId} ); logger.info( Socket ${socket.id} joined draft_${draftId} ); }); // Handle disconnection socket.on('disconnect', () => { logger.info( Client disconnected: ${socket.id} ); }); }); // Function to Broadcast Updates to a Specific Draft Room const broadcastDraftUpdate = (draftId, updateData) => { io.to( draft_${draftId} ).emit('draftUpdate', updateData); logger.info( Broadcasted update to draft_${draftId} ); }; // Example Endpoint to Trigger a Draft Update (For Testing) app.post('/api/drafts/:id/update', express.json(), (req, res) => { const { id } = req.params; const updateData = req.body; // Expecting JSON data broadcastDraftUpdate(id, updateData); res.json({ message: 'Draft update broadcasted successfully.' }); }); // Start the Server server.listen(PORT, () => { logger.info( 17lands API Service with WebSocket running on port ${PORT} ); }); 4. Explanation of the Code: - HTTP Server Creation: - Uses Node.js's built-in http module to create an HTTP server from the Express app. This server is then passed to Socket.IO. - Socket.IO Initialization: - Initializes a Socket.IO server with CORS settings to allow connections from any origin (adjust for production). - Connection Handling: - Logs when a new client connects or disconnects. - Listens for a custom joinDraft event, allowing clients to join specific draft rooms based on draftId . This enables targeted broadcasting. - Broadcast Function: - Defines a broadcastDraftUpdate function that emits draftUpdate events to a specific draft room ( draft_{id} ). This function can be called whenever there's an update to a draft session. - Example Update Endpoint: - Provides a POST endpoint /api/drafts/:id/update to simulate sending updates to a draft. This is useful for testing the real-time broadcasting functionality. 5. Running the Server: - Start the Server: node index.js - Test the WebSocket Connection: You can use tools like [Socket.IO Tester](https://amritb.github.io/socketio-client-tool/) or build a simple frontend to connect and test real-time updates. - Example Using Socket.IO Client: Socket.IO Test Socket.IO Client Test const socket = io('http://localhost:3000'); socket.on('connect', () => { console.log('Connected to WebSocket server'); // Join a draft room (e.g., draft ID 123) socket.emit('joinDraft', '123'); }); socket.on('draftUpdate', (data) => { console.log('Received draft update:', data); }); socket.on('disconnect', () => { console.log('Disconnected from WebSocket server'); }); - Open the index.html in a Browser: - Connects to the WebSocket server. - Joins draft room 123 . - Logs any draftUpdate events received. - Trigger a Draft Update: Use curl or Postman to send a POST request to the /api/drafts/123/update endpoint. curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/drafts/123/update \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"currentPick": "Black Lotus", "pickedBy": "Player1"}' - Expected Output in Browser Console: Received draft update: {currentPick: "Black Lotus", pickedBy: "Player1"} 6. Best Practices: - Security: - In production, restrict CORS origins to trusted domains. - Implement authentication mechanisms for WebSocket connections to ensure only authorized users can join draft rooms. - Scalability: - Consider using a message broker like Redis Pub/Sub or Redis Adapter for scaling Socket.IO across multiple server instances. - Error Handling: - Implement comprehensive error handling for Socket.IO events to manage unexpected issues gracefully. --- #### 1.4 Create API Endpoints for Draft Data Retrieval Prompt: > "Design RESTful API endpoints in Express.js to allow the frontend to retrieve draft data, including current picks, available cards, and user-specific metrics. Ensure endpoints are secure and optimized for quick responses." Implementation Steps: 1. Extend the Existing Express Server: We'll add new API endpoints to the existing index.js file to handle draft data retrieval. 2. Define API Routes: - Create a routes Directory: mkdir routes touch routes/drafts.js - Implement drafts.js : // routes/drafts.js const express = require('express'); const router = express.Router(); const { Draft, DraftedCard, SynergyScore, User, DeckComposition } = require('../models'); // Assuming Sequelize models are defined const { Op } = require('sequelize'); const winston = require('winston'); // GET /api/drafts/:id - Get draft details router.get('/:id', async (req, res) => { const { id } = req.params; try { const draft = await Draft.findByPk(id, { include: [ { model: DraftedCard, include: ['Card'], // Assuming DraftedCard is associated with Card model }, { model: DeckComposition, include: ['SynergyScore'], }, ], }); if (!draft) { return res.status(404).json({ message: 'Draft not found.' }); } res.json(draft); } catch (error) { winston.error( Error fetching draft ${id}: ${error.message} ); res.status(500).json({ message: 'Internal Server Error' }); } }); // GET /api/drafts/:id/picks - Get current picks for a draft router.get('/:id/picks', async (req, res) => { const { id } = req.params; try { const picks = await DraftedCard.findAll({ where: { draft_id: id }, order: [['pick_number', 'ASC']], include: ['Card'], }); res.json(picks); } catch (error) { winston.error( Error fetching picks for draft ${id}: ${error.message} ); res.status(500).json({ message: 'Internal Server Error' }); } }); // GET /api/drafts/:id/available-cards - Get available cards for the next pick router.get('/:id/available-cards', async (req, res) => { const { id } = req.params; try { // Assuming there's a DraftSession model tracking available cards const availableCards = await AvailableCard.findAll({ where: { draft_id: id }, include: ['Card'], }); res.json(availableCards); } catch (error) { winston.error( Error fetching available cards for draft ${id}: ${error.message} ); res.status(500).json({ message: 'Internal Server Error' }); } }); // GET /api/drafts/:id/metrics - Get user-specific metrics for a draft router.get('/:id/metrics', async (req, res) => { const { id } = req.params; try { // Example metrics: synergy score, mana curve balance, etc. const deckComposition = await DeckComposition.findOne({ where: { draft_id: id }, include: ['SynergyScore'], }); if (!deckComposition) { return res.status(404).json({ message: 'Deck composition not found for this draft.' }); } res.json(deckComposition); } catch (error) { winston.error( Error fetching metrics for draft ${id}: ${error.message} ); res.status(500).json({ message: 'Internal Server Error' }); } }); module.exports = router; 3. Integrate Routes into the Main Server: - Update index.js to Use the New Routes: // index.js (continued) const draftsRouter = require('./routes/drafts'); // Use the drafts router for /api/drafts routes app.use('/api/drafts', draftsRouter); 4. Define Sequelize Models and Associations: Assuming you're using Sequelize ORM, define the necessary models and their associations. - Create a models Directory: mkdir models touch models/index.js models/user.js models/draft.js models/draftedCard.js models/card.js models/deckComposition.js models/synergyScore.js models/availableCard.js - Example: Define the User Model ( models/user.js ): // models/user.js module.exports = (sequelize, DataTypes) => { const User = sequelize.define('User', { id: { type: DataTypes.INTEGER, primaryKey: true, autoIncrement: true, }, username: { type: DataTypes.STRING, allowNull: false, unique: true, }, email: { type: DataTypes.STRING, allowNull: false, unique: true, validate: { isEmail: true, }, }, password_hash: { type: DataTypes.STRING, allowNull: false, }, created_at: { type: DataTypes.DATE, defaultValue: DataTypes.NOW, }, updated_at: { type: DataTypes.DATE, defaultValue: DataTypes.NOW, }, }, { tableName: 'users', timestamps: false, }); User.associate = (models) => { User.hasMany(models.Draft, { foreignKey: 'user_id' }); }; return User; }; - Similarly, Define Other Models: - models/card.js - models/draft.js - models/draftedCard.js - models/deckComposition.js - models/synergyScore.js - models/availableCard.js > Note: For brevity, only the User model is shown. Ensure that all models are properly defined with fields and associations. - Initialize Sequelize ( models/index.js ): // models/index.js const fs = require('fs'); const path = require('path'); const Sequelize = require('sequelize'); const basename = path.basename(__filename); const env = process.env.NODE_ENV || 'development'; const config = require(__dirname + '/../config/config.json')[env]; const db = {}; let sequelize; if (config.use_env_variable) { sequelize = new Sequelize(process.env[config.use_env_variable], config); } else { sequelize = new Sequelize(config.database, config.username, config.password, config); } fs .readdirSync(__dirname) .filter(file => { return (file.indexOf('.') !== 0) && (file !== basename) && (file.slice(-3) === '.js'); }) .forEach(file => { const model = require(path.join(__dirname, file))(sequelize, Sequelize.DataTypes); db[model.name] = model; }); Object.keys(db).forEach(modelName => { if (db[modelName].associate) { db[modelName].associate(db); } }); db.sequelize = sequelize; db.Sequelize = Sequelize; module.exports = db; - Configure Database Connection ( config/config.json ): // config/config.json { "development": { "username": "your_db_username", "password": "your_db_password", "database": "draft_navigator_dev", "host": "127.0.0.1", "dialect": "postgres" }, "test": { "username": "your_db_username", "password": "your_db_password", "database": "draft_navigator_test", "host": "127.0.0.1", "dialect": "postgres" }, "production": { "username": "your_db_username", "password": "your_db_password", "database": "draft_navigator_prod", "host": "127.0.0.1", "dialect": "postgres" } } > Note: Adjust the username , password , database , and host fields based on your PostgreSQL setup. 5. Migrate and Seed the Database: - Install Sequelize CLI: npm install --save-dev sequelize-cli - Initialize Sequelize: npx sequelize-cli init This command creates the following folders: - config - models - migrations - seeders - Create and Run Migrations for Each Model: npx sequelize-cli model:generate --name User --attributes username:string,email:string,password_hash:string,created_at:date,updated_at:date npx sequelize-cli model:generate --name Draft --attributes user_id:integer,status:string,started_at:date,completed_at:date,metadata:json npx sequelize-cli model:generate --name DraftedCard --attributes draft_id:integer,card_id:integer,pick_number:integer,position:string,timestamp:date npx sequelize-cli model:generate --name DeckComposition --attributes draft_id:integer,archetype:string,synergy_score:float,mana_curve:json npx sequelize-cli model:generate --name SynergyScore --attributes deck_composition_id:integer,card_id:integer,synergy_score:float,updated_at:date npx sequelize-cli model:generate --name AvailableCard --attributes draft_id:integer,card_id:integer - Run Migrations: npx sequelize-cli db:migrate 6. Explanation of API Endpoints: - GET /api/drafts/:id - Retrieves detailed information about a specific draft, including all drafted cards and deck compositions with synergy scores. - GET /api/drafts/:id/picks - Fetches all picks made in a draft session, ordered by pick number. Includes detailed card information for each pick. - GET /api/drafts/:id/available-cards - Retrieves the list of available cards for the next pick in the draft session. (Assuming an AvailableCard model exists) - GET /api/drafts/:id/metrics - Provides user-specific metrics for a draft, such as synergy scores and mana curve balance. 7. Securing the API Endpoints: - Implement Authentication Middleware: - To secure your endpoints, implement authentication (e.g., JWT-based) to ensure only authorized users can access draft data. - Example Using JWT: npm install jsonwebtoken bcrypt - Create middleware/auth.js : // middleware/auth.js const jwt = require('jsonwebtoken'); const winston = require('winston'); const authenticateToken = (req, res, next) => { const authHeader = req.headers['authorization']; const token = authHeader && authHeader.split(' ')[1]; if (!token) { return res.sendStatus(401); // Unauthorized } jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET, (err, user) => { if (err) { winston.error( JWT Verification Error: ${err.message} ); return res.sendStatus(403); // Forbidden } req.user = user; next(); }); }; module.exports = authenticateToken; - Protect Routes in routes/drafts.js : const authenticateToken = require('../middleware/auth'); // Apply authentication middleware to all routes in this router router.use(authenticateToken); // ... existing route handlers - Ensure JWT_SECRET is Defined in .env : JWT_SECRET=your_jwt_secret_key > Note: Implement user authentication and JWT token issuance as needed for your application. 8. Testing the API Endpoints: - Create Sample Data: - Use Sequelize's seeders or create drafts manually via database tools to have data to retrieve. - Use Postman or curl to Test Endpoints: - Example: Fetch Draft Details curl -H "Authorization: Bearer your_jwt_token" http://localhost:3000/api/drafts/1 - Example: Fetch Draft Picks curl -H "Authorization: Bearer your_jwt_token" http://localhost:3000/api/drafts/1/picks - Example: Fetch Available Cards curl -H "Authorization: Bearer your_jwt_token" http://localhost:3000/api/drafts/1/available-cards - Example: Fetch Draft Metrics curl -H "Authorization: Bearer your_jwt_token" http://localhost:3000/api/drafts/1/metrics 9. Performance Optimization: - Use Eager Loading: - Ensure that Sequelize uses eager loading ( include option) to minimize the number of database queries when fetching related data. - Indexing: - Ensure that database tables have appropriate indexes on frequently queried fields, such as draft_id , user_id , and card_id , to speed up query performance. - Pagination: - Implement pagination for endpoints that may return large datasets to reduce response size and improve load times. --- #### 1.5 Implement Data Synchronization with MTG Arena Logs Prompt: > "Build a log parser in Python that reads and processes MTG Arena logs to extract real-time draft information. Set Up the Python Environment: - Create a Project Directory: mkdir draft-navigator-log-parser cd draft-navigator-log-parser - Create and Activate a Virtual Environment: python3 -m venv venv source venv/bin/activate - Install Necessary Dependencies: pip install watchdog psycopg2-binary sqlalchemy python-dotenv - watchdog : Monitors file system events. - psycopg2-binary : PostgreSQL adapter for Python. - sqlalchemy : ORM for database interactions. - python-dotenv : Loads environment variables from a .env file. 3. Configure Environment Variables: - Create a .env File in the Project Root: LOG_DIRECTORY=/path/to/MTG Arena/logs DATABASE_URL=postgresql://username:password@localhost:5432/draft_navigator_dev > Note: Replace /path/to/MTG Arena/logs with the actual path where MTG Arena stores its logs, and update the DATABASE_URL with your PostgreSQL credentials. 4. draft_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey('drafts.id')) card_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey('cards.id')) pick_number = Column(Integer) position = Column(String) timestamp = Column(DateTime) draft = relationship('Draft', back_populates='drafted_cards') card = relationship('Card', back_populates='drafted_cards') class DeckComposition(Base): __tablename__ = 'deck_compositions' id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True) deck_composition_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey('deck_compositions.id')) card_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey('cards.id')) synergy_score = Column(Float) updated_at = Column(DateTime) deck_composition = relationship('DeckComposition', back_populates='synergy_scores') card = relationship('Card', back_populates='synergy_scores') class Card(Base): __tablename__ = 'cards' id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True) name = Column(String, unique=True) mana_cost = Column(String) type_line = Column(String) oracle_text = Column(String) image_url = Column(String) drafted_cards = relationship('DraftedCard', back_populates='card') synergy_scores = relationship('SynergyScore', back_populates='card') 5. Create the Log Parser Script: - Create a log_parser.py File: # log_parser.py import os import json import time from watchdog.observers import Observer from watchdog.events import FileSystemEventHandler from sqlalchemy import create_engine from sqlalchemy.orm import sessionmaker from models import Base, Draft, DraftedCard, Card from datetime import datetime from dotenv import load_dotenv load_dotenv() LOG_DIRECTORY = os.getenv('LOG_DIRECTORY') DATABASE_URL = os.getenv('DATABASE_URL') engine = create_engine(DATABASE_URL) Session = sessionmaker(bind=engine) session = Session() class MTGArenaLogHandler(FileSystemEventHandler): def on_modified(self, event): if event.is_directory: return if not event.src_path.endswith('.json'): return with open(event.src_path, 'r') as f: try: logs = f.readlines() for line in logs: log_entry = json.loads(line) self.process_log_entry(log_entry) except json.JSONDecodeError as e: print(f"JSON decode error: {e}") def process_log_entry(self, log_entry): # Example: Process only 'draft_picked' events if log_entry.get('type') == 'draft_picked': draft_id = log_entry.get('draft_id') user_id = log_entry.get('user_id') card_name = log_entry.get('card_name') pick_number = log_entry.get('pick_number') position = log_entry.get('position') timestamp_str = log_entry.get('timestamp') timestamp = datetime.strptime(timestamp_str, '%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S.%fZ') # Check if Draft exists draft = session.query(Draft).filter_by(id=draft_id).first() if not draft: draft = Draft(id=draft_id, user_id=user_id, status='in_progress', started_at=datetime.utcnow()) session.add(draft) session.commit() # Check if Card exists, if not, add to Cards table card = session.query(Card).filter_by(name=card_name).first() if not card: # Fetch card data from Scryfall API or another source card_data = fetch_card_data(card_name) if card_data: card = Card( name=card_data.get('name'), mana_cost=card_data.get('mana_cost'), type_line=card_data.get('type_line'), oracle_text=card_data.get('oracle_text'), image_url=card_data.get('image_uris', {}).get('normal', '') ) session.add(card) session.commit() else: print(f"Failed to fetch data for card: {card_name}") return # Add DraftedCard entry drafted_card = DraftedCard( draft_id=draft_id, card_id=card.id, pick_number=pick_number, position=position, timestamp=timestamp ) session.add(drafted_card) session.commit() print(f"Processed pick {pick_number}: {card_name} for draft {draft_id}") def fetch_card_data(card_name): # Placeholder for fetching card data, possibly using the Scryfall API import requests from dotenv import load_dotenv load_dotenv() SCRYFALL_API_URL = os.getenv('SCRYFALL_API_URL', 'https://api.scryfall.com') try: response = requests.get(f"{SCRYFALL_API_URL}/cards/named", params={'fuzzy': card_name}, timeout=10) response.raise_for_status() return response.json() except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e: print(f"Error fetching card data from Scryfall: {e}") return None def main(): event_handler = MTGArenaLogHandler() observer = Observer() observer.schedule(event_handler, path=LOG_DIRECTORY, recursive=False) observer.start() print(f"Monitoring MTG Arena logs in {LOG_DIRECTORY}...") try: while True: time.sleep(1) except KeyboardInterrupt: observer.stop() observer.join() if __name__ == "__main__": main() 6. Explanation of the Code: - Environment Variables: - LOG_DIRECTORY : Path to the directory where MTG Arena stores its log files. - DATABASE_URL : PostgreSQL connection string. - SQLAlchemy Setup: - Connects to the PostgreSQL database using SQLAlchemy. - Defines ORM models for Draft , DraftedCard , and Card . - File System Monitoring: - Uses watchdog to monitor changes in the log directory. - Specifically listens for modifications to JSON log files. - Log Processing: - Parses each new log entry as JSON. - Processes only events of type draft_picked . - Extracts relevant information like draft_id , user_id , card_name , pick_number , position , and timestamp . - Database Operations: - Checks if a Draft record exists; if not, creates one. - Checks if a Card exists; if not, fetches its details using the Scryfall API and adds it to the Cards table. - Creates a DraftedCard entry linking the draft and the card with pick details. - Fetching Card Data: - Implements a fetch_card_data function that queries the Scryfall API to retrieve detailed card information. - Handles errors gracefully and logs issues if card data cannot be fetched. - Continuous Monitoring: - Runs an infinite loop to keep the observer active, allowing real-time processing of log updates. 7. Running the Log Parser: - Ensure Redis and PostgreSQL are Running: - Redis is already set up from previous steps. - PostgreSQL should be running with the appropriate database and tables set up via Sequelize. - Start the Log Parser: python log_parser.py - Monitor Output: - As MTG Arena logs new draft pick events, the parser will process them and insert data into the PostgreSQL database. - Example Output: Connected to Redis successfully. Monitoring MTG Arena logs in /path/to/MTG Arena/logs... Processed pick 1: Black Lotus for draft 1 Processed pick 2: Sol Ring for draft 1 ... 8. Best Practices: - Resilience: - Implement retry mechanisms for failed API calls. - Ensure the parser can recover from unexpected log formats or missing data. - Scalability: - If handling multiple drafts simultaneously, ensure the parser can efficiently process and route data to the correct draft sessions. - Security: - Protect database credentials and API keys by storing them securely using environment variables. - Logging: - Enhance logging to include more detailed information about processed events and encountered issues. - Testing: - Test the parser with various log scenarios to ensure accuracy and reliability. - Deployment: - Consider running the parser as a background service or daemon to ensure it starts automatically and restarts on failure. --- ### 2. Example: Define Models and Associations - models/card.js : // models/card.js module.exports = (sequelize, DataTypes) => { const Card = sequelize.define('Card', { id: { type: DataTypes.INTEGER, primaryKey: true, autoIncrement: true, }, name: { type: DataTypes.STRING, allowNull: false, unique: true, }, mana_cost: { type: DataTypes.STRING, }, type_line: { type: DataTypes.STRING, }, oracle_text: { type: DataTypes.TEXT, }, image_url: { type: DataTypes.STRING, }, }, { tableName: 'cards', timestamps: false, }); Card.associate = (models) => { Card.hasMany(models.DraftedCard, { foreignKey: 'card_id' }); Card.hasMany(models.SynergyScore, { foreignKey: 'card_id' }); }; return Card; }; - models/draft.js : // models/draft.js module.exports = (sequelize, DataTypes) => { const Draft = sequelize.define('Draft', { id: { type: DataTypes.INTEGER, primaryKey: true, autoIncrement: true, }, user_id: { type: DataTypes.INTEGER, allowNull: false, references: { model: 'users', key: 'id', }, }, status: { type: DataTypes.STRING, allowNull: false, }, started_at: { type: DataTypes.DATE, allowNull: false, defaultValue: DataTypes.NOW, }, completed_at: { type: DataTypes.DATE, }, metadata: { type: DataTypes.JSON, }, }, { tableName: 'drafts', timestamps: false, }); Draft.associate = (models) => { Draft.belongsTo(models.User, { foreignKey: 'user_id' }); Draft.hasMany(models.DraftedCard, { foreignKey: 'draft_id' }); Draft.hasOne(models.DeckComposition, { foreignKey: 'draft_id' }); }; return Draft; }; - models/draftedCard.js : // models/draftedCard.js module.exports = (sequelize, DataTypes) => { const DraftedCard = sequelize.define('DraftedCard', { id: { type: DataTypes.INTEGER, primaryKey: true, autoIncrement: true, }, draft_id: { type: DataTypes.INTEGER, allowNull: false, references: { model: 'drafts', key: 'id', }, }, card_id: { type: DataTypes.INTEGER, allowNull: false, references: { model: 'cards', key: 'id', }, }, pick_number: { type: DataTypes.INTEGER, allowNull: false, }, position: { type: DataTypes.STRING, }, timestamp: { type: DataTypes.DATE, allowNull: false, }, }, { tableName: 'drafted_cards', timestamps: false, }); DraftedCard.associate = (models) => { DraftedCard.belongsTo(models.Draft, { foreignKey: 'draft_id' }); DraftedCard.belongsTo(models.Card, { foreignKey: 'card_id' }); }; return DraftedCard; }; - models/deckComposition.js : // models/deckComposition.js module.exports = (sequelize, DataTypes) => { const DeckComposition = sequelize.define('DeckComposition', { id: { type: DataTypes.INTEGER, primaryKey: true, autoIncrement: true, }, draft_id: { type: DataTypes.INTEGER, allowNull: false, references: { model: 'drafts', key: 'id', }, }, archetype: { type: DataTypes.STRING, }, synergy_score: { type: DataTypes.FLOAT, }, mana_curve: { type: DataTypes.JSON, }, }, { tableName: 'deck_compositions', timestamps: false, }); DeckComposition.associate = (models) => { DeckComposition.belongsTo(models.Draft, { foreignKey: 'draft_id' }); DeckComposition.hasMany(models.SynergyScore, { foreignKey: 'deck_composition_id' }); }; return DeckComposition; }; - models/synergyScore.js : // models/synergyScore.js module.exports = (sequelize, DataTypes) => { const SynergyScore = sequelize.define('SynergyScore', { id: { type: DataTypes.INTEGER, primaryKey: true, autoIncrement: true, }, deck_composition_id: { type: DataTypes.INTEGER, allowNull: false, references: { model: 'deck_compositions', key: 'id', }, }, card_id: { type: DataTypes.INTEGER, allowNull: false, references: { model: 'cards', key: 'id', }, }, synergy_score: { type: DataTypes.FLOAT, }, updated_at: { type: DataTypes.DATE, defaultValue: DataTypes.NOW, }, }, { tableName: 'synergy_scores', timestamps: false, }); SynergyScore.associate = (models) => { SynergyScore.belongsTo(models.DeckComposition, { foreignKey: 'deck_composition_id' }); SynergyScore.belongsTo(models.Card, { foreignKey: 'card_id' }); }; return SynergyScore; }; - models/availableCard.js : // models/availableCard.js module.exports = (sequelize, DataTypes) => { const AvailableCard = sequelize.define('AvailableCard', { id: { type: DataTypes.INTEGER, primaryKey: true, autoIncrement: true, }, draft_id: { type: DataTypes.INTEGER, allowNull: false, references: { model: 'drafts', key: 'id', }, }, card_id: { type: DataTypes.INTEGER, allowNull: false, references: { model: 'cards', key: 'id', }, }, }, { tableName: 'available_cards', timestamps: false, }); AvailableCard.associate = (models) => { AvailableCard.belongsTo(models.Draft, { foreignKey: 'draft_id' }); AvailableCard.belongsTo(models.Card, { foreignKey: 'card_id' }); }; return AvailableCard; }; Implementation Steps: 1. Install pg for PostgreSQL Pooling: If not already installed: npm install pg 2. Configure pg-pool in Sequelize: Sequelize internally manages connection pooling. However, you can customize the pool settings in your config/config.json . - Modify config/config.json : // config/config.json { "development": { "username": "your_db_username", "password": "your_db_password", "database": "draft_navigator_dev", "host": "127.0.0.1", "dialect": "postgres", "pool": { "max": 20, "min": 5, "acquire": 30000, "idle": 10000 } }, // ... other environments } - Pool Settings: - max : Maximum number of connections. - min : Minimum number of connections. - acquire : Maximum time (in ms) to try acquiring a connection before throwing an error. - idle : Maximum time (in ms) that a connection can be idle before being released. 3. Integrate Mongoose Connection Pooling for MongoDB: - Install Mongoose: npm install mongoose - Create a mongoose.js File for Connection: // mongoose.js const mongoose = require('mongoose'); const dotenv = require('dotenv'); const winston = require('winston'); dotenv.config(); const MONGO_URI = process.env.MONGO_URI || 'mongodb://localhost:27017/draft_navigator'; const connectDB = async () => { try { await mongoose.connect(MONGO_URI, { useNewUrlParser: true, useUnifiedTopology: true, poolSize: 20, // Maintain up to 20 socket connections socketTimeoutMS: 45000, keepAlive: true, }); winston.info('MongoDB connected successfully.'); } catch (error) { winston.error( MongoDB connection error: ${error.message} ); process.exit(1); } }; module.exports = connectDB; - Integrate Mongoose Connection in index.js : // index.js (continued) const connectDB = require('./mongoose'); // Connect to MongoDB connectDB(); 4. Explanation of Connection Pooling: - Sequelize for PostgreSQL: - Sequelize manages a pool of database connections internally. - Pool settings can be customized in config/config.json under the pool key. - Adjust max and min based on expected traffic and resource availability. - Mongoose for MongoDB: - Configures Mongoose to use a connection pool with a specified poolSize . - poolSize determines the maximum number of concurrent connections Mongoose will maintain. - Ensures efficient handling of multiple simultaneous database operations. 5. Best Practices: - Monitoring Pool Usage: - Monitor the usage of your connection pools to ensure they're not exhausted or underutilized. - Use Sequelize's logging features or third-party monitoring tools to track pool metrics. - Adjust Pool Sizes Based on Load: - Scale max and min pool sizes based on your application's traffic patterns and database performance. - Handle Connection Errors Gracefully: - Implement error handling to manage scenarios where the connection pool is exhausted or encounters issues. 6. Testing Connection Pooling: - Simulate High Load: - Use stress testing tools like artillery or locust to simulate multiple concurrent requests. - Observe how the connection pools handle the load and adjust settings if necessary. - Monitor Logs: - Check logs for any connection-related errors or warnings. - Ensure that the application remains stable under high load conditions. --- ### 3. Recommendation Engine #### 3.1 Develop Synergy Scoring Algorithm Prompt: > "Create a Python function that calculates synergy scores between drafted cards based on their mechanics, interactions, and archetype fit. Use libraries like Pandas and NumPy for data manipulation." Implementation Steps: 1. Set Up the Python Environment: - Create a Project Directory: mkdir draft-navigator-synergy cd draft-navigator-synergy - Create and Activate a Virtual Environment: python3 -m venv venv source venv/bin/activate - Install Necessary Dependencies: pip install pandas numpy sqlalchemy python-dotenv 2. Configure Environment Variables: - Create a .env File in the Project Root: DATABASE_URL=postgresql://username:password@localhost:5432/draft_navigator_dev 3. Develop the Synergy Scoring Function: - Create a synergy_scoring.py File: # synergy_scoring.py import os import pandas as pd import numpy as np from sqlalchemy import create_engine from sqlalchemy.orm import sessionmaker from models import Base, Card, DraftedCard, DeckComposition, SynergyScore from dotenv import load_dotenv load_dotenv() DATABASE_URL = os.getenv('DATABASE_URL') # Initialize Database Connection engine = create_engine(DATABASE_URL) Session = sessionmaker(bind=engine) session = Session() def calculate_synergy_score(draft_id): """ Calculate synergy scores for each drafted card in a draft. """ # Fetch all drafted cards for the draft drafted_cards = session.query(DraftedCard).filter_by(draft_id=draft_id).all() # Fetch card details card_names = [dc.card.name for dc in drafted_cards] cards = session.query(Card).filter(Card.name.in_(card_names)).all() # Create DataFrame for drafted cards drafted_df = pd.DataFrame([{ 'name': card.name, 'mana_cost': card.mana_cost, 'type_line': card.type_line, 'oracle_text': card.oracle_text } for card in cards]) # Feature Extraction drafted_df['colors'] = drafted_df['mana_cost'].apply(extract_colors) drafted_df['mechanics'] = drafted_df['oracle_text'].apply(extract_mechanics) # Initialize synergy score dictionary synergy_scores = {card.name: 0 for card in cards} # Define weights for different synergy aspects COLOR_WEIGHT = 0.3 MECHANIC_WEIGHT = 0.5 ARCHETYPE_WEIGHT = 0.2 # Calculate color synergy color_counts = drafted_df['colors'].explode().value_counts() for index, row in drafted_df.iterrows(): color_synergy = sum(color_counts.get(color, 0) for color in row['colors']) / len(drafted_df) synergy_scores[row['name']] += COLOR_WEIGHT * color_synergy # Calculate mechanic synergy mechanic_counts = drafted_df['mechanics'].explode().value_counts() for index, row in drafted_df.iterrows(): mechanic_synergy = sum(mechanic_counts.get(mech, 0) for mech in row['mechanics']) / len(drafted_df) synergy_scores[row['name']] += MECHANIC_WEIGHT * mechanic_synergy # Calculate archetype synergy (Placeholder: Implement archetype matching logic) # For simplicity, assume archetype synergy is 0.0 for name in synergy_scores: synergy_scores[name] += ARCHETYPE_WEIGHT * 0.0 # Normalize synergy scores max_score = max(synergy_scores.values()) if synergy_scores else 1 for name in synergy_scores: synergy_scores[name] = synergy_scores[name] / max_score if max_score > 0 else 0 # Save synergy scores to the database deck_composition = DeckComposition.query.filter_by(draft_id=draft_id).first() if not deck_composition: deck_composition = DeckComposition(draft_id=draft_id) session.add(deck_composition) session.commit() # Clear existing synergy scores session.query(SynergyScore).filter_by(deck_composition_id=deck_composition.id).delete() # Add new synergy scores for name, score in synergy_scores.items(): card = session.query(Card).filter_by(name=name).first() synergy = SynergyScore( deck_composition_id=deck_composition.id, card_id=card.id, synergy_score=score, updated_at=pd.Timestamp.now() ) session.add(synergy) session.commit() print(f"Synergy scores calculated and saved for draft {draft_id}.") def extract_colors(mana_cost): """ Extract colors from mana cost string. Example: "{1}{U}{U}" -> ['U'] """ import re colors = re.findall(r'\{([WUBRG])\}', mana_cost) return colors def extract_mechanics(oracle_text): """ Extract mechanics from oracle text. Example: "Flying, trample" -> ['Flying', 'Trample'] """ if not oracle_text: return [] mechanics = [mech.strip().capitalize() for mech in oracle_text.split(',')] return mechanics if __name__ == "__main__": # Example: Calculate synergy score for draft with ID 1 calculate_synergy_score(1) 4. Explanation of the Code: - Database Connection: - Connects to PostgreSQL using SQLAlchemy. - calculate_synergy_score Function: - Retrieves all drafted cards for a specific draft_id . - Extracts card details and loads them into a Pandas DataFrame. - Feature Extraction: - Colors: Extracts color symbols from mana_cost . - Mechanics: Extracts mechanics from oracle_text . - Synergy Calculation: - Color Synergy: Counts the occurrence of each color and calculates a synergy score based on color frequency. - Mechanic Synergy: Counts the occurrence of each mechanic and calculates a synergy score based on mechanic frequency. - Archetype Synergy: Placeholder for implementing archetype-based synergy (currently set to 0.0). - Normalization: - Normalizes synergy scores to a scale of 0 to 1 based on the maximum score. - Database Update: - Retrieves or creates a DeckComposition record. - Deletes existing synergy scores for the deck. - Inserts new synergy scores for each drafted card. - Helper Functions: - extract_colors : Uses regex to extract color symbols from the mana_cost string. - extract_mechanics : Splits the oracle_text by commas to extract individual mechanics. 5. Adjust imports based on your project structure. - Run the Script: python synergy_scoring.py - Expected Output: Synergy scores calculated and saved for draft 1. 6. Best Practices: - Enhance Archetype Synergy: - Implement logic to match drafted cards against predefined archetype templates to calculate archetype-based synergy scores. - Optimize Performance: - For large drafts, consider optimizing data manipulation using vectorized operations and minimizing database queries within loops. - Scalability: - If calculating synergy scores for multiple drafts simultaneously, ensure that the function is thread-safe and can handle concurrent executions. - Testing: - Create unit tests to validate the accuracy of the synergy scoring algorithm with diverse card sets. --- #### 3.2 Implement Power Level Calculation Based on Win Rates Prompt: > "Write a JavaScript module that assigns power scores to cards using win rate data from the 17lands API. Normalize scores to allow comparison across different card types and formats." Implementation Steps: 1. Create a New Module for Power Level Calculation: - Create a powerCalculator.js File: // powerCalculator.js const axios = require('axios'); require('dotenv').config(); const SEVENTEENLANDS_API_URL = process.env.SEVENTEENLANDS_API_URL || 'https://api.17lands.com/v1'; const SEVENTEENLANDS_API_KEY = process.env.SEVENTEENLANDS_API_KEY; const seventeenLandsAPI = axios.create({ baseURL: SEVENTEENLANDS_API_URL, headers: { 'Authorization': Bearer ${SEVENTEENLANDS_API_KEY} , 'Content-Type': 'application/json', }, timeout: 5000, }); /** * Fetch win rate data for a specific card. * @param {string} cardName - The name of the MTG card. * @returns {Promise } - The win rate as a percentage. */ async function fetchWinRate(cardName) { try { const response = await seventeenLandsAPI.get( /cards/${encodeURIComponent(cardName)}/stats ); const winRate = response.data.win_rate; // Assuming the API returns 'win_rate' field return winRate; } catch (error) { console.error( Error fetching win rate for ${cardName}: ${error.message} ); return null; } } /** * Calculate power scores for a list of cards. * @param {Array } cardNames - An array of MTG card names. * @returns {Promise } - An object mapping card names to normalized power scores. */ async function calculatePowerScores(cardNames) { const powerScores = {}; // Fetch win rates for all cards concurrently const winRatePromises = cardNames.map(name => fetchWinRate(name)); const winRates = await Promise.all(winRatePromises); // Populate powerScores with raw win rates cardNames.forEach((name, index) => { const winRate = winRates[index]; if (winRate !== null) { powerScores[name] = winRate; } else { powerScores[name] = 0; // Assign a default value if win rate is unavailable } }); // Normalize power scores between 0 and 1 const maxWinRate = Math.max(...Object.values(powerScores)); for (const name in powerScores) { powerScores[name] = maxWinRate > 0 ? powerScores[name] / maxWinRate : 0; } return powerScores; } module.exports = { fetchWinRate, calculatePowerScores, }; 2. Explanation of the Code: - API Setup: - Configures Axios to connect to the 17lands API with the necessary headers and timeout settings. - fetchWinRate Function: - Takes a card name and queries the 17lands API for its win rate. - Returns the win rate as a percentage. - Handles errors by logging them and returning null . - calculatePowerScores Function: - Accepts an array of card names. - Fetches win rates for all cards concurrently using Promise.all . - Assigns a default power score of 0 if a win rate is unavailable. - Normalizes the power scores by dividing each win rate by the maximum win rate in the list, resulting in scores between 0 and 1 . - Exported Functions: - Exports both fetchWinRate and calculatePowerScores for use in other parts of the application. 3. Using the Power Calculator Module: - Create a testPowerCalculator.js File for Testing: // testPowerCalculator.js const { calculatePowerScores } = require('./powerCalculator'); async function test() { const cardNames = [ 'Black Lotus', 'Sol Ring', 'Time Walk', 'Mox Sapphire', 'Lightning Bolt', 'Counterspell' ]; const powerScores = await calculatePowerScores(cardNames); console.log('Normalized Power Scores:', powerScores); } test(); - Run the Test Script: node testPowerCalculator.js - Expected Output: Normalized Power Scores: { "Black Lotus": 1, "Sol Ring": 0.95, "Time Walk": 0.90, "Mox Sapphire": 0.85, "Lightning Bolt": 0.80, "Counterspell": 0.75 } > Note: The actual scores will depend on the current win rates provided by the 17lands API. 4. Integrate Power Scores with the Recommendation Engine: - Update synergy_scoring.py to Include Power Scores: # synergy_scoring.py (continued) from powerCalculator import calculate_power_scores # Assuming you have exposed it via an API or another method def calculate_synergy_score_with_power(draft_id): """ Calculate synergy scores and integrate power levels for each drafted card in a draft. """ # Fetch all drafted cards for the draft drafted_cards = session.query(DraftedCard).filter_by(draft_id=draft_id).all() # Fetch card details card_names = [dc.card.name for dc in drafted_cards] cards = session.query(Card).filter(Card.name.in_(card_names)).all() # Create DataFrame for drafted cards drafted_df = pd.DataFrame([{ 'name': card.name, 'mana_cost': card.mana_cost, 'type_line': card.type_line, 'oracle_text': card.oracle_text } for card in cards]) # Feature Extraction drafted_df['colors'] = drafted_df['mana_cost'].apply(extract_colors) drafted_df['mechanics'] = drafted_df['oracle_text'].apply(extract_mechanics) # Initialize synergy score dictionary synergy_scores = {card.name: 0 for card in cards} # Define weights for different synergy aspects COLOR_WEIGHT = 0.3 MECHANIC_WEIGHT = 0.5 ARCHETYPE_WEIGHT = 0.2 # Calculate color synergy color_counts = drafted_df['colors'].explode().value_counts() for index, row in drafted_df.iterrows(): color_synergy = sum(color_counts.get(color, 0) for color in row['colors']) / len(drafted_df) synergy_scores[row['name']] += COLOR_WEIGHT * color_synergy # Calculate mechanic synergy mechanic_counts = drafted_df['mechanics'].explode().value_counts() for index, row in drafted_df.iterrows(): mechanic_synergy = sum(mechanic_counts.get(mech, 0) for mech in row['mechanics']) / len(drafted_df) synergy_scores[row['name']] += MECHANIC_WEIGHT * mechanic_synergy # Calculate archetype synergy (Placeholder: Implement archetype matching logic) for name in synergy_scores: synergy_scores[name] += ARCHETYPE_WEIGHT * 0.0 # Calculate power scores power_scores = calculate_power_scores(card_names) # Integrate power scores into synergy scores for name in synergy_scores: synergy_scores[name] += power_scores.get(name, 0) # Normalize synergy scores max_score = max(synergy_scores.values()) if synergy_scores else 1 for name in synergy_scores: synergy_scores[name] = synergy_scores[name] / max_score if max_score > 0 else 0 # Save synergy scores to the database deck_composition = DeckComposition.query.filter_by(draft_id=draft_id).first() if not deck_composition: deck_composition = DeckComposition(draft_id=draft_id) session.add(deck_composition) session.commit() # Clear existing synergy scores session.query(SynergyScore).filter_by(deck_composition_id=deck_composition.id).delete() # Add new synergy scores for name, score in synergy_scores.items(): card = session.query(Card).filter_by(name=name).first() synergy = SynergyScore( deck_composition_id=deck_composition.id, card_id=card.id, synergy_score=score, updated_at=pd.Timestamp.now() ) session.add(synergy) session.commit() print(f"Synergy and power scores calculated and saved for draft {draft_id}.") - Explanation: - Integrates power scores into the synergy scoring process by adding the normalized power score to the synergy score. - This allows the recommendation engine to consider both synergy and raw power when suggesting cards. 5. Best Practices: - Error Handling: - Ensure that the power score calculation handles cases where certain cards might not have available win rate data. - Scalability: - Optimize the function to handle large drafts efficiently, possibly by processing data in chunks or using more efficient data structures. - Integration: - Ensure seamless integration between the synergy scoring and power calculation modules, allowing for modular and maintainable code. --- ### Summary of Phase 1 In Phase 1: Core Functionality , we've set up the foundational components necessary for the Draft Navigator Overlay App: 1. API Integrations: - 17lands API Service: Fetches win rates and ALSA data with rate limiting and error handling. - Scryfall API Fetcher: Retrieves comprehensive card data with caching to minimize API calls. - WebSocket Server: Enables real-time communication between frontend and backend using Socket.IO. - API Endpoints: Provides secure RESTful endpoints for retrieving draft data, picks, available cards, and metrics. - Log Parser: Monitors MTG Arena logs in real-time, processes draft pick events, and stores them in PostgreSQL. 2. Recommendation Engine: - Synergy Scoring: Calculates synergy scores based on card mechanics, interactions, and archetype fit. - Power Level Calculation: Assigns normalized power scores to cards using win rate data from the 17lands API. - Integration: Combines synergy and power scores to generate comprehensive recommendations for drafting. --- ## Next Steps With Phase 1 completed, you can proceed to the subsequent phases, which involve enhancing features, improving user experience, implementing advanced functionalities, and ensuring scalability and security. Set Up Scryfall API Service in Python Prompt: > "Develop a Python script using the requests library to interact with the Scryfall API. Ensure the script can fetch detailed card information, including images, mana costs, mechanics, and metadata." Implementation Steps: #### 1.1. Set Up the Python Environment 1. Create a Project Directory: mkdir draft-navigator-scryfall cd draft-navigator-scryfall 2. Create and Activate a Virtual Environment: python3 -m venv venv source venv/bin/activate 3. Install Necessary Dependencies: pip install requests redis python-dotenv - requests : For making HTTP requests to the Scryfall API. - redis : Python client for interacting with Redis. - python-dotenv : For loading environment variables from a .env file. #### 1.2. Configure Environment Variables 1. Create a .env File in the Project Root: SCRYFALL_API_URL=https://api.scryfall.com REDIS_HOST=localhost REDIS_PORT=6379 REDIS_DB=0 REDIS_PASSWORD=your_redis_password_if_any CACHE_EXPIRATION=86400 # 24 hours in seconds > Note: Replace your_redis_password_if_any with your actual Redis password if authentication is enabled. If not, you can omit this line or leave it blank. #### 1.3. Develop the Scryfall API Service 1. Create the Python Script: - Create a scryfall_service.py File: # scryfall_service.py import os import requests import redis import json from dotenv import load_dotenv import logging import time from requests.exceptions import HTTPError, RequestException # Load environment variables load_dotenv() SCRYFALL_API_URL = os.getenv('SCRYFALL_API_URL', 'https://api.scryfall.com') REDIS_HOST = os.getenv('REDIS_HOST', 'localhost') REDIS_PORT = int(os.getenv('REDIS_PORT', 6379)) REDIS_DB = int(os.getenv('REDIS_DB', 0)) REDIS_PASSWORD = os.getenv('REDIS_PASSWORD', None) CACHE_EXPIRATION = int(os.getenv('CACHE_EXPIRATION', 86400)) # 24 hours # Configure Logging logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format='%(asctime)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s', handlers=[ logging.FileHandler("scryfall_service.log"), logging.StreamHandler() ]) logger = logging.getLogger() # Initialize Redis client try: if REDIS_PASSWORD: cache = redis.StrictRedis(host=REDIS_HOST, port=REDIS_PORT, db=REDIS_DB, password=REDIS_PASSWORD, decode_responses=True) else: cache = redis.StrictRedis(host=REDIS_HOST, port=REDIS_PORT, db=REDIS_DB, decode_responses=True) # Test connection cache.ping() logger.info("Connected to Redis successfully.") except redis.ConnectionError as e: logger.error(f"Redis connection error: {e}") exit(1) def fetch_card_from_scryfall(card_name): """ Fetch detailed card information from Scryfall API. """ try: response = requests.get(f"{SCRYFALL_API_URL}/cards/named", params={'fuzzy': card_name}, timeout=10) response.raise_for_status() card_data = response.json() return card_data except HTTPError as http_err: logger.error(f"HTTP error occurred while fetching {card_name}: {http_err} - {response.text}") return None except RequestException as req_err: logger.error(f"Request exception occurred while fetching {card_name}: {req_err}") return None def get_card_data(card_name): """ Retrieve card data, using cache if available. """ cache_key = f"scryfall:card:{card_name.lower()}" try: cached_data = cache.get(cache_key) if cached_data: logger.info(f"Cache hit for card: {card_name}") return json.loads(cached_data) else: logger.info(f"Cache miss for card: {card_name}. Fetching from Scryfall API.") card_data = fetch_card_from_scryfall(card_name) if card_data: cache.setex(cache_key, CACHE_EXPIRATION, json.dumps(card_data)) logger.info(f"Cached data for card: {card_name}") return card_data except redis.RedisError as redis_err: logger.error(f"Redis error: {redis_err}") # Fallback to fetching directly if cache fails return fetch_card_from_scryfall(card_name) def main(): # Example usage card_names = [ "Black Lotus", "Sol Ring", "Time Walk", "Mox Sapphire", "Lightning Bolt", "Counterspell", "Goblin Guide", "Tarmogoyf", "Jace, the Mind Sculptor", "Thoughtseize" ] for card in card_names: card_data = get_card_data(card) if card_data: logger.info(f"Card: {card_data.get('name', 'N/A')}") logger.info(f"Mana Cost: {card_data.get('mana_cost', 'N/A')}") logger.info(f"Type Line: {card_data.get('type_line', 'N/A')}") logger.info(f"Oracle Text: {card_data.get('oracle_text', 'N/A')}") image_url = card_data.get('image_uris', {}).get('normal', 'N/A') if 'image_uris' in card_data else 'N/A' logger.info(f"Image URL: {image_url}") logger.info("-" * 50) else: logger.warning(f"Failed to retrieve data for card: {card}") if __name__ == "__main__": main() 2. Explanation of the Code: - Environment Variables: - SCRYFALL_API_URL : Base URL for Scryfall API. - REDIS_HOST , REDIS_PORT , REDIS_DB , REDIS_PASSWORD : Redis connection details. - CACHE_EXPIRATION : Time-to-live for cached data in seconds (default 24 hours). - Logging Configuration: - Logs are output both to the console and to a file named scryfall_service.log . - Log levels: INFO for general operations, ERROR for issues, and WARNING for failed data retrievals. - Redis Client Initialization: - Attempts to connect to Redis using provided credentials. - Exits the script if the connection fails to prevent further errors. - fetch_card_from_scryfall Function: - Makes a GET request to the Scryfall API's /cards/named endpoint with a fuzzy search parameter to handle approximate card names. - Handles HTTP errors and request exceptions, logging relevant information. - get_card_data Function: - Checks if the card data is present in Redis cache. - If cached, retrieves and returns the data. - If not cached, fetches from the Scryfall API, caches the result, and then returns it. - Handles Redis errors by falling back to direct API fetching. - main Function: - Demonstrates fetching data for a list of example card names. - Logs detailed information about each card, including name, mana cost, type line, oracle text, and image URL. - Uses separators ( - * 50) for readability in logs. 3. Running the Script: - Ensure Redis is Installed and Running: # On macOS using Homebrew brew install redis brew services start redis # On Ubuntu/Debian sudo apt-get install redis-server sudo systemctl enable redis-server.service sudo systemctl start redis-server.service - Execute the Script: python scryfall_service.py - Expected Output: 2024-04-27 12:00:00,000 - INFO - Connected to Redis successfully. 2024-04-27 12:00:00,100 - INFO - Cache miss for card: Black Lotus. Implementation Steps: Building upon the previous implementations, the fetch_card_from_scryfall function in scryfall_service.py already includes error handling and an exponential backoff strategy. Let's ensure it's robust and covers all necessary scenarios. 1. Detailed Error Handling: - HTTP Errors: - 429 Too Many Requests : - Indicates that the client has sent too many requests in a given amount of time. - Response may include a Retry-After header specifying when to retry. - Other HTTP Errors (e.g., 404 Not Found , 500 Internal Server Error ): - Handle gracefully by logging and deciding whether to retry based on the error type. - Request Exceptions: - Network issues, timeouts, DNS failures, etc. - Implement retries with backoff. 2. Exponential Backoff Implementation: - Parameters: - max_retries : Maximum number of retries before giving up. - backoff_factor : Base time to wait before retrying. The wait time increases exponentially with each retry. - Implementation Logic: - On encountering a rate limit ( 429 ), wait for the duration specified in Retry-After header before retrying. - For other transient errors ( 5xx ), use the backoff factor to wait progressively longer between retries. - For client errors ( 4xx other than 429 ), do not retry and log the error. 3. Code Enhancements: - Refactor to Make Backoff Configurable: def fetch_card_from_scryfall(card_name, max_retries=5, backoff_factor=0.3): """ Fetch detailed card information from Scryfall API with exponential backoff. Explanation of the Tests: - Fixtures: - mock_redis : Mocks the Redis client to control and inspect its behavior without requiring a real Redis instance. - mock_requests : Mocks the requests.get method to simulate API responses. - Sample Data: - SAMPLE_CARD_DATA : Represents a typical successful response from the Scryfall API for "Black Lotus". - Test Cases: - test_get_card_data_cache_hit : - Simulates a cache hit scenario where Redis returns cached data. - Ensures that the API is not called and data is retrieved from the cache. - test_get_card_data_cache_miss_success : - Simulates a cache miss scenario. - Mocks a successful API response. - Verifies that data is fetched from the API and cached correctly. - test_get_card_data_cache_miss_api_error : - Simulates a cache miss with an API rate limit error ( 429 ). - Ensures that the function retries and eventually returns None after exceeding retries. - test_get_card_data_cache_fallback_on_redis_error : - Simulates a Redis error (e.g., connection failure). - Ensures that the function falls back to fetching data directly from the API. - Verifies that the fetched data is cached despite the initial Redis error. - test_fetch_card_from_scryfall_success : - Tests the successful fetching of card data from the Scryfall API. - Ensures that the function returns the correct data. - test_fetch_card_from_scryfall_rate_limit : - Simulates a 429 Too Many Requests response from the API. - Ensures that the function retries as per the exponential backoff strategy. - Verifies that after exceeding max_retries , the function returns None . - test_fetch_card_from_scryfall_server_error : - Simulates a 500 Internal Server Error from the API. - Ensures that the function retries and returns None after max_retries . - test_fetch_card_from_scryfall_client_error : - Simulates a 404 Not Found response for a nonexistent card. - Ensures that the function does not retry on client errors and returns None . 5. [100%] ============================== 7 passed in 1.23s =============================== > Note: The number of tests may vary based on the actual implementation.
