Best Practices: - Mock External Dependencies: - Use mocking to simulate Redis and API responses, ensuring tests are fast and reliable without depending on external services. - Isolate Tests: - Each test should be independent and not rely on the state or outcome of other tests. - Comprehensive Coverage: - Ensure that all possible scenarios, including edge cases and error conditions, are covered by tests. - Use Fixtures for Reusability: - Utilize PyTest fixtures to set up common test environments, reducing code duplication and improving maintainability. --- ## Summary of Phase 1.2 In Phase 1.2: Integrate Scryfall API for Comprehensive Card Data , we've achieved the following: 1. Initialize Socket.IO in Node.js Backend Prompt: > "Install and configure Socket.IO in your Node.js backend to establish real-time communication channels between the server and frontend clients." Implementation Steps: #### 1.1. Install Socket.IO Dependencies Ensure that you have already installed socket.io on the backend. If not, execute the following command: npm install socket.io Additionally, for enhanced security, it's recommended to use HTTPS with Socket.IO. Install https and fs (if not already available): npm install https #### 1.2. Configure Socket.IO in Your Server Assuming you're extending the existing index.js file where Socket.IO was partially set up, we'll enhance its configuration. Updated index.js : // index.js require('dotenv').config(); const express = require('express'); const axios = require('axios'); const rateLimit = require('express-rate-limit'); const winston = require('winston'); const http = require('http'); const { Server } = require('socket.io'); const fs = require('fs'); const path = require('path'); const app = express(); const PORT = process.env.PORT || 3000; // Configure Winston Logger const logger = winston.createLogger({ level: 'info', format: winston.format.combine( winston.format.timestamp(), winston.format.json() ), transports: [ new winston.transports.Console(), new winston.transports.File({ filename: 'error.log', level: 'error' }), new winston.transports.File({ filename: 'combined.log' }), ], }); // Rate Limiting Middleware const limiter = rateLimit({ windowMs: parseInt(process.env.RATE_LIMIT_WINDOW_MS) || 60000, // 1 minute max: parseInt(process.env.RATE_LIMIT_MAX) || 60, // limit each IP to 60 requests per windowMs message: 'Too many requests from this IP, please try again after a minute.', handler: (req, res, next, options) => { logger.warn( Rate limit exceeded for IP: ${req.ip} ); res.status(options.statusCode).json({ message: options.message }); }, }); app.use(limiter); // 17lands API Service const seventeenLandsAPI = axios.create({ baseURL: process.env.SEVENTEENLANDS_API_URL, headers: { 'Authorization': Bearer ${process.env.SEVENTEENLANDS_API_KEY} , 'Content-Type': 'application/json', }, timeout: 5000, // 5 seconds timeout }); // Endpoint to Fetch Win Rates and ALSA Data for a Specific Card app.get('/api/cards/:cardName/stats', async (req, res) => { const { cardName } = req.params; try { const response = await seventeenLandsAPI.get( /cards/${encodeURIComponent(cardName)}/stats ); res.json(response.data); } catch (error) { if (error.response) { // API responded with a status outside 2xx logger.error( 17lands API Error: ${error.response.status} - ${error.response.data} ); res.status(error.response.status).json({ message: error.response.data }); } else if (error.request) { // No response received logger.error( 17lands API No Response: ${error.message} ); res.status(503).json({ message: '17lands API is unavailable. Please try again later.' }); } else { // Other errors logger.error( Error fetching 17lands data: ${error.message} ); res.status(500).json({ message: 'Internal Server Error' }); } } }); // Create HTTP Server const server = http.createServer(app); // Initialize Socket.IO Server const io = new Server(server, { cors: { origin: '*', // Important: Restrict this in production for security methods: ['GET', 'POST'], }, }); // Handle Socket.IO Connections io.on('connection', (socket) => { logger.info( New client connected: ${socket.id} ); // Event: joinDraft - Join a specific draft room socket.on('joinDraft', (draftId) => { socket.join( draft_${draftId} ); logger.info( Socket ${socket.id} joined draft_${draftId} ); socket.emit('joinedDraft', { draftId, message: Joined draft ${draftId} }); }); // Event: leaveDraft - Leave a specific draft room socket.on('leaveDraft', (draftId) => { socket.leave( draft_${draftId} ); logger.info( Socket ${socket.id} left draft_${draftId} ); socket.emit('leftDraft', { draftId, message: Left draft ${draftId} }); }); // Event: updateDraft - Receive draft updates from client socket.on('updateDraft', (data) => { const { draftId, update } = data; logger.info( Received update for draft ${draftId} from socket ${socket.id}: ${JSON.stringify(update)} ); // Broadcast the update to other clients in the same draft room socket.to( draft_${draftId} ).emit('draftUpdated', { draftId, update }); }); // Event: sendRecommendations - Receive recommendations from server // (Typically, the server would emit recommendations rather than receive them, but included per prompt) socket.on('sendRecommendations', (data) => { const { draftId, recommendations } = data; logger.info( Received recommendations for draft ${draftId} from socket ${socket.id}: ${JSON.stringify(recommendations)} ); // Broadcast recommendations to other clients in the same draft room socket.to( draft_${draftId} ).emit('recommendationsReceived', { draftId, recommendations }); }); // Handle disconnection socket.on('disconnect', () => { logger.info( Client disconnected: ${socket.id} ); }); }); // Function to Broadcast Updates to a Specific Draft Room const broadcastDraftUpdate = (draftId, updateData) => { io.to( draft_${draftId} ).emit('draftUpdate', updateData); logger.info( Broadcasted update to draft_${draftId}: ${JSON.stringify(updateData)} ); }; // Example Endpoint to Trigger a Draft Update (For Testing) app.post('/api/drafts/:id/update', express.json(), (req, res) => { const { id } = req.params; const updateData = req.body; // Expecting JSON data broadcastDraftUpdate(id, updateData); res.json({ message: 'Draft update broadcasted successfully.' }); }); // Start the Server server.listen(PORT, () => { logger.info( 17lands API Service with WebSocket running on port ${PORT} ); }); #### 1.3. Explanation of the Enhancements: 1. HTTPS Configuration (Optional but Recommended): For secure WebSocket communications ( wss ), it's recommended to serve your Socket.IO server over HTTPS. Here's how to set it up: - Obtain SSL Certificates: - You can use services like [Let's Encrypt](https://letsencrypt.org/) for free SSL certificates. - For development purposes, you can generate self-signed certificates. - Modify index.js to Use HTTPS: const https = require('https'); // Path to your SSL certificate and key const sslOptions = { key: fs.readFileSync(path.join(__dirname, 'certs', 'key.pem')), cert: fs.readFileSync(path.join(__dirname, 'certs', 'cert.pem')), }; // Create HTTPS Server const httpsServer = https.createServer(sslOptions, app); // Initialize Socket.IO with HTTPS Server const io = new Server(httpsServer, { cors: { origin: '*', // Important: Restrict this in production for security methods: ['GET', 'POST'], }, }); // Start the HTTPS Server httpsServer.listen(PORT, () => { logger.info( 17lands API Service with WebSocket (HTTPS) running on port ${PORT} ); }); > Note: Ensure that your SSL certificates ( key.pem and cert.pem ) are correctly placed in the specified directory. 2. Socket.IO Event Handlers: - joinDraft : - Allows clients to join a specific draft session. - Joins the client to a room named draft_{draftId} . - Emits a confirmation event joinedDraft back to the client. - leaveDraft : - Allows clients to leave a specific draft session. - Removes the client from the room draft_{draftId} . - Emits a confirmation event leftDraft back to the client. - updateDraft : - Receives updates related to a draft from a client. - Broadcasts the update to all other clients in the same draft room via the draftUpdated event. - sendRecommendations : - Receives recommendation data from a client. - Broadcasts the recommendations to other clients in the same draft room via the recommendationsReceived event. - Note: Typically, recommendations would be generated by the server and emitted to clients. Adjust this as per your application's logic. - disconnect : - Logs when a client disconnects. 3. Broadcasting Real-Time Data: - broadcastDraftUpdate Function: - Simplifies broadcasting updates to a specific draft room. - Emits the draftUpdate event with the provided data to all clients in draft_{draftId} . 4. Managing Multiple Concurrent Connections: - Namespaces and Rooms: - Namespaces: Allow segregation of communication channels based on specific paths (e.g., /draft namespace). - Rooms: Facilitate grouping of sockets within a namespace, enabling targeted broadcasting. - Implementation Using Rooms: - Each draft session is represented as a room named draft_{draftId} . - Clients join or leave these rooms based on the draft they are participating in. - This ensures that updates are only sent to relevant clients. 5. Secure WebSocket Communications: - CORS Configuration: - Restrict the origin in the CORS settings to trusted domains in production. - Example: const io = new Server(server, { cors: { origin: 'https://your-frontend-domain.com', methods: ['GET', 'POST'], }, }); - Authentication: - Implement authentication to ensure that only authorized users can connect to the WebSocket server. - Using JWT (JSON Web Tokens): - Clients must provide a valid JWT token upon connection. - Validate the token on the server before allowing the connection. - Data Sanitization: - Validate and sanitize all incoming data to prevent injection attacks. - Use libraries like [validator.js](https://github.com/validatorjs/validator.js) for data validation. - Using Secure Protocols (WSS): - Serve Socket.IO over HTTPS to encrypt data in transit. - This prevents man-in-the-middle attacks and eavesdropping. - Example: Implementing JWT Authentication with Socket.IO: const jwt = require('jsonwebtoken'); // Middleware for authenticating Socket.IO connections io.use((socket, next) => { const token = socket.handshake.auth.token; if (!token) { logger.warn( Socket ${socket.id} missing authentication token. ); return next(new Error('Authentication error')); } jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET, (err, decoded) => { if (err) { logger.warn( Socket ${socket.id} provided invalid token. ); return next(new Error('Authentication error')); } socket.user = decoded; // Attach decoded token data to socket next(); }); }); io.on('connection', (socket) => { logger.info( Authenticated client connected: ${socket.id} (User ID: ${socket.user.id}) ); // ... existing event handlers ... }); - Client-Side Connection Example with JWT: // client.js (Frontend) import { io } from "socket.io-client"; const socket = io('https://your-backend-domain.com', { auth: { token: 'your_jwt_token_here' } }); socket.on('connect', () => { console.log( Connected with ID: ${socket.id} ); // Join a draft room after connecting socket.emit('joinDraft', '12345'); }); socket.on('connect_error', (err) => { console.error( Connection error: ${err.message} ); }); > Note: Replace 'your_jwt_token_here' with the actual JWT token obtained after user authentication. #### 1.4. Complete Example: Enhanced index.js with Security Measures Here's how your index.js file might look after incorporating authentication and other security measures: // index.js require('dotenv').config(); logger.info( Broadcasted update to draft_${draftId}: ${JSON.stringify(updateData)} ); }; // Example Endpoint to Trigger a Draft Update (For Testing) app.post('/api/drafts/:id/update', express.json(), (req, res) => { const { id } = req.params; const updateData = req.body; // Expecting JSON data // Validate draftId if (!validator.isAlphanumeric(id)) { return res.status(400).json({ message: 'Invalid draft ID.' }); } // Sanitize updateData if necessary broadcastDraftUpdate(id, updateData); res.json({ message: 'Draft update broadcasted successfully.' }); }); // Start the Server server.listen(PORT, () => { logger.info( 17lands API Service with WebSocket running on port ${PORT} ); }); Key Enhancements: 1. Authentication Middleware: - JWT Verification: - Validates the JWT token provided by the client during the handshake. - Attaches decoded user information to the socket object for further use. 2. Data Validation and Sanitization: - validator.js : - Utilized to validate and sanitize incoming data, such as draftId . - Prevents injection attacks and ensures data integrity. 3. Error Handling: - Emits error events back to the client if invalid data is detected. - Logs warnings and errors for invalid operations. 4. Secure CORS Configuration: - Restricts the allowed origins in production to trusted frontend domains. - Example: cors: { origin: 'https://your-frontend-domain.com', methods: ['GET', 'POST'], }, 5. Namespace Implementation (Optional): - For further segregation, you can implement namespaces (e.g., /draft ). - Example: const draftNamespace = io.of('/draft'); draftNamespace.use((socket, next) => { // Authentication logic }); draftNamespace.on('connection', (socket) => { // Event handlers specific to the draft namespace }); - Note: Namespaces are beneficial when you have different communication channels for various parts of your application. #### 1.5. Best Practices for WebSocket Implementation 1. Restrict CORS Origins: - In production, always specify trusted origins to prevent unauthorized access. - Example: cors: { origin: 'https://your-frontend-domain.com', methods: ['GET', 'POST'], }, 2. Implement Authentication: - Ensure that only authenticated users can establish WebSocket connections. - Utilize tokens (e.g., JWT) to verify user identity. 3. Use Secure Protocols (WSS): - Serve your Socket.IO server over HTTPS to encrypt data in transit. - Prevents eavesdropping and man-in-the-middle attacks. 4. Data Validation and Sanitization: - Validate all incoming data to prevent injection attacks. - Use libraries like validator.js for robust validation. 5. Limit Room Access: - Ensure that clients can only join rooms they are authorized to access. - Prevent clients from joining arbitrary rooms. 6. Handle Disconnections Gracefully: - Clean up any resources or references when clients disconnect. - Ensure that the server remains stable even with frequent connect/disconnect events. 7. Monitor and Log Activities: - Keep detailed logs of connection events, errors, and data broadcasts. - Use logs for monitoring, debugging, and security audits. 8. Scalability Considerations: - If expecting high traffic, consider deploying Socket.IO with multiple instances. - Use adapters like Redis Adapter to enable horizontal scaling. npm install @socket.io/redis-adapter redis - Example Setup: const { createAdapter } = require('@socket.io/redis-adapter'); const { createClient } = require('redis'); const pubClient = createClient({ host: 'localhost', port: 6379 }); const subClient = pubClient.duplicate(); io.adapter(createAdapter(pubClient, subClient)); > Note: Ensure Redis is properly configured and accessible. --- ### 2. Create WebSocket Event Handlers Prompt: > "Develop event handlers for key WebSocket events such as connect , disconnect , updateDraft , and sendRecommendations . Ensure each handler performs the necessary logic for real-time updates." Implementation Steps: #### 2.1. Define and Implement Event Handlers In the enhanced index.js , we've already implemented several event handlers. Let's ensure each handler performs the necessary logic effectively. 1. connect Event: - Automatically handled by Socket.IO. - Custom logic can be added within the connection event handler. 2. disconnect Event: - Handles client disconnections. - Can be used to perform cleanup tasks if necessary. 3. joinDraft Event: - Allows clients to join a specific draft room. - Ensures that only authorized clients can join. 4. leaveDraft Event: - Allows clients to leave a draft room. - Useful for managing user participation dynamically. 5. updateDraft Event: - Receives updates related to a draft from clients. - Broadcasts these updates to other clients in the same draft room. 6. sendRecommendations Event: - Receives recommendation data from clients (though typically recommendations are server-generated). - Broadcasts recommendations to other clients in the same draft room. #### 2.2. Detailed Implementation of Each Event Handler joinDraft Event: socket.on('joinDraft', (draftId) => { // Validate draftId (ensure it's a valid format) if (!validator.isAlphanumeric(draftId)) { logger.warn( Invalid draftId provided by socket ${socket.id}: ${draftId} ); return socket.emit('error', { message: 'Invalid draft ID.' }); } socket.join( draft_${draftId} ); logger.info( Socket ${socket.id} joined draft_${draftId} ); socket.emit('joinedDraft', { draftId, message: Joined draft ${draftId} }); }); - Purpose: Allows a client to join a specific draft session. - Validation: Ensures draftId is alphanumeric to prevent injection attacks. - Action: Joins the client to a room named draft_{draftId} and emits a confirmation event. leaveDraft Event: socket.on('leaveDraft', (draftId) => { if (!validator.isAlphanumeric(draftId)) { logger.warn( Invalid draftId provided by socket ${socket.id}: ${draftId} ); return socket.emit('error', { message: 'Invalid draft ID.' }); } socket.leave( draft_${draftId} ); logger.info( Socket ${socket.id} left draft_${draftId} ); socket.emit('leftDraft', { draftId, message: Left draft ${draftId} }); }); - Purpose: Allows a client to leave a specific draft session. - Validation: Ensures draftId is alphanumeric. - Action: Removes the client from the room and emits a confirmation event. updateDraft Event: socket.on('updateDraft', (data) => { const { draftId, update } = data; // Validate draftId if (!validator.isAlphanumeric(draftId)) { logger.warn( Invalid draftId in updateDraft from socket ${socket.id}: ${draftId} ); return socket.emit('error', { message: 'Invalid draft ID.' }); } // Sanitize update data (example: ensure update is an object) if (typeof update !== 'object' || Array.isArray(update)) { logger.warn( Invalid update data from socket ${socket.id}: ${JSON.stringify(update)} ); return socket.emit('error', { message: 'Invalid update data format.' }); } logger.info( Received update for draft ${draftId} from socket ${socket.id}: ${JSON.stringify(update)} ); // Broadcast the update to other clients in the same draft room socket.to( draft_${draftId} ).emit('draftUpdated', { draftId, update }); }); - Purpose: Receives draft-related updates from a client and broadcasts them to other participants. - Validation: - Ensures draftId is valid. - Validates that update is an object. - Action: Broadcasts the update to all other clients in the same draft room. sendRecommendations Event: socket.on('sendRecommendations', (data) => { const { draftId, recommendations } = data; // Validate draftId if (!validator.isAlphanumeric(draftId)) { logger.warn( Invalid draftId in sendRecommendations from socket ${socket.id}: ${draftId} ); return socket.emit('error', { message: 'Invalid draft ID.' }); } // Sanitize recommendations (example: ensure it's an array) if (!Array.isArray(recommendations)) { logger.warn( Invalid recommendations format from socket ${socket.id}: ${JSON.stringify(recommendations)} ); return socket.emit('error', { message: 'Invalid recommendations format.' }); } logger.info( Received recommendations for draft ${draftId} from socket ${socket.id}: ${JSON.stringify(recommendations)} ); // Broadcast recommendations to other clients in the same draft room socket.to( draft_${draftId} ).emit('recommendationsReceived', { draftId, recommendations }); }); - Purpose: Receives recommendation data from a client and broadcasts it to other participants. - Note: Typically, recommendations are generated server-side. Adjust this event based on your application's logic. - Validation: - Ensures draftId is valid. - Validates that recommendations is an array. - Action: Broadcasts the recommendations to all other clients in the same draft room. disconnect Event: socket.on('disconnect', () => { logger.info( Client disconnected: ${socket.id} ); }); - Purpose: Handles client disconnections. - Action: Logs the disconnection event. Additional cleanup can be performed here if necessary. --- ### 3. Implement Broadcasting of Real-Time Data Prompt: > "Set up the server to broadcast updated draft data, synergy scores, and recommendations to all connected clients using Socket.IO's emit and broadcast methods." Implementation Steps: #### 3.1. Define Broadcasting Functions To streamline broadcasting, define utility functions that handle emitting events to specific rooms. Example: // Function to broadcast updated draft data const broadcastDraftData = (draftId, draftData) => { io.to( draft_${draftId} ).emit('draftDataUpdated', draftData); logger.info( Broadcasted draft data update to draft_${draftId} ); }; // Function to broadcast synergy scores const broadcastSynergyScores = (draftId, synergyScores) => { io.to( draft_${draftId} ).emit('synergyScoresUpdated', synergyScores); logger.info( Broadcasted synergy scores update to draft_${draftId} ); }; // Function to broadcast recommendations const broadcastRecommendations = (draftId, recommendations) => { io.to( draft_${draftId} ).emit('recommendationsUpdated', recommendations); logger.info( Broadcasted recommendations update to draft_${draftId} ); }; #### 3.2. Utilize Broadcasting Functions in Your Application Logic Whenever there's an update to the draft data, synergy scores, or recommendations (e.g., after a draft pick), invoke the corresponding broadcasting function. Example Integration: Assuming you have functions that handle draft logic, such as adding a new pick, calculating synergy scores, and generating recommendations, integrate broadcasting within these functions. // Example function to handle a new draft pick const handleNewDraftPick = async (draftId, cardName, userId) => { // Logic to add the new pick to the database // ... // Fetch updated draft data const updatedDraftData = await getDraftData(draftId); // Implement this function // Broadcast updated draft data broadcastDraftData(draftId, updatedDraftData); // Calculate updated synergy scores const updatedSynergyScores = await calculateSynergyScores(draftId); // Implement this function // Broadcast updated synergy scores broadcastSynergyScores(draftId, updatedSynergyScores); // Generate new recommendations const newRecommendations = await generateRecommendations(draftId); // Implement this function // Broadcast new recommendations broadcastRecommendations(draftId, newRecommendations); }; #### 3.3. Example Client-Side Handling On the frontend, handle the incoming broadcasted events to update the UI in real-time. Example (Using JavaScript on the Frontend): // client.js (Frontend) import { io } from "socket.io-client"; const socket = io('https://your-backend-domain.com', { auth: { token: 'your_jwt_token_here' } }); // Join a draft room after connecting socket.on('connect', () => { console.log( Connected with ID: ${socket.id} ); const draftId = '12345'; // Example draft ID socket.emit('joinDraft', draftId); }); // Handle draft data updates socket.on('draftDataUpdated', (draftData) => { console.log('Draft Data Updated:', draftData); // Update UI accordingly }); // Handle synergy scores updates socket.on('synergyScoresUpdated', (synergyScores) => { console.log('Synergy Scores Updated:', synergyScores); // Update UI accordingly }); // Handle recommendations updates socket.on('recommendationsUpdated', (recommendations) => { console.log('Recommendations Updated:', recommendations); // Update UI accordingly }); // Handle joinedDraft confirmation socket.on('joinedDraft', (data) => { console.log(data.message); }); // Handle errors socket.on('error', (error) => { console.error('Socket.IO Error:', error.message); }); --- ### 4. Manage Multiple Concurrent Connections Efficiently Prompt: > "Optimize your WebSocket server to handle multiple simultaneous connections. Use namespaces or rooms in Socket.IO to segment different draft sessions and ensure scalability." Implementation Steps: #### 4.1. Utilize Rooms for Segregating Draft Sessions Rooms in Socket.IO allow you to broadcast messages to a subset of connected clients. This is ideal for managing multiple draft sessions simultaneously. Implementation Overview: - Room Naming Convention: - Use a consistent naming pattern, such as draft_{draftId} , to represent each draft session. - Joining Rooms: - Clients join specific rooms based on the draft they are participating in. - Broadcasting Within Rooms: - Emit events to a specific room to ensure only relevant clients receive the updates. Example: // Emitting an event to a specific room io.to( draft_${draftId} ).emit('eventName', data); // Broadcasting to all clients except the sender in a room socket.to( draft_${draftId} ).emit('eventName', data); #### 4.2. Scaling Socket.IO with Multiple Instances If you anticipate a high volume of connections, consider scaling your Socket.IO server horizontally across multiple instances. Steps to Implement Scaling: 1. Use Redis Adapter: The Redis adapter enables multiple Socket.IO server instances to communicate through Redis, ensuring that events are properly broadcasted across all instances. - Install Dependencies: npm install @socket.io/redis-adapter redis - Configure Redis Adapter in index.js : const { createAdapter } = require('@socket.io/redis-adapter'); const { createClient } = require('redis'); // Create Redis clients for pub and sub const pubClient = createClient({ host: process.env.REDIS_HOST, port: process.env.REDIS_PORT, password: process.env.REDIS_PASSWORD }); const subClient = pubClient.duplicate(); // Connect Redis clients pubClient.connect().catch(console.error); subClient.connect().catch(console.error); // Use Redis adapter io.adapter(createAdapter(pubClient, subClient)); 2. Deploy Multiple Server Instances: - Use process managers like [PM2](https://pm2.keymetrics.io/) to run multiple instances of your Node.js server. - Install PM2: npm install -g pm2 - Start Multiple Instances: pm2 start index.js -i max - The -i max flag tells PM2 to run as many instances as there are CPU cores. 3. Load Balancing: - Use a load balancer (e.g., Nginx, HAProxy) to distribute incoming traffic across multiple server instances. - Example Nginx Configuration: http { upstream socketio_servers { server 127.0.0.1:3000; server 127.0.0.1:3001; # Add more servers as needed } server { listen 443 ssl; server_name your-backend-domain.com; ssl_certificate /path/to/cert.pem; ssl_certificate_key /path/to/key.pem; location /socket.io/ { proxy_pass http://socketio_servers; proxy_http_version 1.1; proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade; proxy_set_header Connection "Upgrade"; proxy_set_header Host $host; } location / { proxy_pass http://socketio_servers; proxy_http_version 1.1; proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade; proxy_set_header Connection "Upgrade"; proxy_set_header Host $host; } } } - Note: Ensure that sticky sessions are enabled if required, although with the Redis adapter, it's less of a concern. 4. Monitoring and Performance Optimization: - Monitor server performance and resource usage using tools like [New Relic](https://newrelic.com/), [Datadog](https://www.datadoghq.com/), or built-in PM2 monitoring. - Optimize event handling and minimize data payload sizes to reduce server load. --- ### 5. Secure WebSocket Communications Prompt: > "Implement security measures for your WebSocket server, such as validating incoming connections, using secure protocols (wss), and sanitizing data to prevent injection attacks." Implementation Steps: #### 5.1. Enforce Secure Protocols (WSS) Serving your WebSocket connections over HTTPS ensures that data transmitted between clients and the server is encrypted. 1. Obtain SSL Certificates: - Use services like [Let's Encrypt](https://letsencrypt.org/) for free SSL certificates. - For development, you can create self-signed certificates: mkdir certs openssl req -nodes -new -x509 -keyout certs/key.pem -out certs/cert.pem -days 365 2. Configure HTTPS Server with Socket.IO: - Update index.js : const https = require('https'); // Path to SSL certificate and key const sslOptions = { key: fs.readFileSync(path.join(__dirname, 'certs', 'key.pem')), cert: fs.readFileSync(path.join(__dirname, 'certs', 'cert.pem')), }; // Create HTTPS Server const httpsServer = https.createServer(sslOptions, app); // Initialize Socket.IO with HTTPS Server const io = new Server(httpsServer, { cors: { origin: process.env.CLIENT_ORIGIN || '*', // Replace '*' with your frontend domain in production methods: ['GET', 'POST'], }, }); // ... existing Socket.IO setup ... // Start the HTTPS Server httpsServer.listen(PORT, () => { logger.info( 17lands API Service with WebSocket (HTTPS) running on port ${PORT} ); }); - Update Client-Side Connection: // client.js (Frontend) const socket = io('https://your-backend-domain.com', { auth: { token: 'your_jwt_token_here' } }); - Note: Ensure that your SSL certificates are correctly configured and that your domain points to the server. #### 5.2. Implement Authentication and Authorization 1. Use JWT for Authenticating Connections: - Backend ( index.js ): const jwt = require('jsonwebtoken'); // Middleware for authenticating Socket.IO connections io.use((socket, next) => { const token = socket.handshake.auth.token; if (!token) { logger.warn( Socket ${socket.id} missing authentication token. ); return next(new Error('Authentication error')); } jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET, (err, decoded) => { if (err) { logger.warn( Socket ${socket.id} provided invalid token. ); return next(new Error('Authentication error')); } socket.user = decoded; // Attach decoded token data to socket next(); }); }); - Frontend ( client.js ): const socket = io('https://your-backend-domain.com', { auth: { token: 'your_jwt_token_here' } }); - Explanation: - Clients must provide a valid JWT token during the handshake. - The server verifies the token and attaches user information to the socket object. - If authentication fails, the connection is rejected. 2. Role-Based Access Control (Optional): - Implement roles (e.g., admin, user) within your JWT payload. - Restrict certain actions or rooms based on user roles. Example: io.use((socket, next) => { const token = socket.handshake.auth.token; if (!token) { return next(new Error('Authentication error')); } jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET, (err, decoded) => { if (err) { return next(new Error('Authentication error')); } // Check user role if (decoded.role !== 'admin') { // Restrict access to certain rooms or actions socket.admin = false; } else { socket.admin = true; } socket.user = decoded; next(); }); }); - Usage: - Within event handlers, check socket.admin to determine if the user has admin privileges. #### 5.3. Data Validation and Sanitization 1. Use validator.js for Input Validation: - Ensures that all incoming data adheres to expected formats and types. - Example: const isValidDraftId = (draftId) => validator.isAlphanumeric(draftId); const isValidUpdate = (update) => typeof update === 'object' && !Array.isArray(update); const isValidRecommendations = (recommendations) => Array.isArray(recommendations); 2. Sanitize Incoming Data: - Prevent injection attacks by sanitizing strings and objects. - Example: const sanitizeString = (str) => validator.escape(str); const sanitizeObject = (obj) => { const sanitized = {}; for (const key in obj) { if (typeof obj[key] === 'string') { sanitized[key] = validator.escape(obj[key]); } else { sanitized[key] = obj[key]; } } return sanitized; }; 3. Implement Sanitization in Event Handlers: - Example: socket.on('updateDraft', (data) => { let { draftId, update } = data; // Validate draftId if (!isValidDraftId(draftId)) { logger.warn( Invalid draftId in updateDraft from socket ${socket.id}: ${draftId} ); return socket.emit('error', { message: 'Invalid draft ID.' }); } // Sanitize update data update = sanitizeObject(update); logger.info( Received update for draft ${draftId} from socket ${socket.id}: ${JSON.stringify(update)} ); // Broadcast the sanitized update to other clients in the same draft room socket.to( draft_${draftId} ).emit('draftUpdated', { draftId, update }); }); #### 5.4. Prevent Unauthorized Room Access Ensure that clients can only join rooms (draft sessions) they are authorized to participate in. Implementation Strategies: 1. Server-Side Verification: - Before allowing a client to join a room, verify that the client has permission to access that draft. - Example: socket.on('joinDraft', async (draftId) => { if (!isValidDraftId(draftId)) { logger.warn( Invalid draftId provided by socket ${socket.id}: ${draftId} ); return socket.emit('error', { message: 'Invalid draft ID.' }); } // Check if the user is authorized to join the draft const isAuthorized = await verifyUserDraftAccess(socket.user.id, draftId); // Implement this function if (!isAuthorized) { logger.warn( Unauthorized draft access attempt by socket ${socket.id} for draft ${draftId} ); return socket.emit('error', { message: 'Unauthorized access to this draft.' }); } socket.join( draft_${draftId} ); logger.info( Socket ${socket.id} joined draft_${draftId} ); socket.emit('joinedDraft', { draftId, message: Joined draft ${draftId} }); }); - Note: Implement the verifyUserDraftAccess function to check if the user is part of the draft session. 2. Restrict Emitting Events to Authorized Users: - Ensure that only users authorized to perform certain actions can emit specific events. - Example: socket.on('sendRecommendations', (data) => { const { draftId, recommendations } = data; if (!isValidDraftId(draftId)) { logger.warn( Invalid draftId in sendRecommendations from socket ${socket.id}: ${draftId} ); return socket.emit('error', { message: 'Invalid draft ID.' }); } // Verify if the user has the authority to send recommendations if (!socket.admin) { // Assuming only admins can send recommendations logger.warn( Unauthorized recommendation attempt by socket ${socket.id} for draft ${draftId} ); return socket.emit('error', { message: 'Unauthorized to send recommendations.' }); } // Sanitize recommendations if (!isValidRecommendations(recommendations)) { logger.warn( Invalid recommendations format from socket ${socket.id}: ${JSON.stringify(recommendations)} ); return socket.emit('error', { message: 'Invalid recommendations format.' }); } logger.info( Received recommendations for draft ${draftId} from socket ${socket.id}: ${JSON.stringify(recommendations)} ); // Broadcast recommendations to other clients in the same draft room socket.to( draft_${draftId} ).emit('recommendationsReceived', { draftId, recommendations }); }); --- ### 6. Finalizing Security Measures 1. Sanitize All Outgoing Data: - Ensure that any data sent to clients is sanitized to prevent XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) attacks. - Example: const sanitizeOutgoingData = (data) => { if (typeof data === 'string') { return validator.escape(data); } else if (typeof data === 'object') { return sanitizeObject(data); } return data; }; // Use in broadcasting functions const broadcastDraftUpdate = (draftId, updateData) => { const sanitizedData = sanitizeOutgoingData(updateData); io.to( draft_${draftId} ).emit('draftUpdate', sanitizedData); logger.info( Broadcasted sanitized update to draft_${draftId} ); }; 2. Implement Content Security Policy (CSP): - Restrict the sources from which resources can be loaded on the frontend. - Example (Express Middleware): const helmet = require('helmet'); app.use(helmet({ contentSecurityPolicy: { directives: { defaultSrc: ["'self'"], scriptSrc: ["'self'", "https://cdn.socket.io"], connectSrc: ["'self'", "https://your-backend-domain.com"], imgSrc: ["'self'", "https://c1.scryfall.com"], // Allow images from Scryfall styleSrc: ["'self'", "'unsafe-inline'"], // Add other directives as needed }, }, })); > Note: Install helmet if not already installed: > > npm install helmet > 3. Implement Rate Limiting for WebSocket Events: - Prevent abuse by limiting the number of specific events a client can emit within a timeframe. - Example: const EventEmitterRateLimiter = (maxEvents, windowMs) => { const clients = {}; return (socket, next) => { socket.on('updateDraft', (data) => { const clientId = socket.id; const currentTime = Date.now(); if (!clients[clientId]) { clients[clientId] = []; } // Remove events older than windowMs clients[clientId] = clients[clientId].filter(timestamp => currentTime - timestamp = maxEvents) { logger.warn( Rate limit exceeded for socket ${clientId} ); socket.emit('error', { message: 'Rate limit exceeded. Please slow down.' }); return; } clients[clientId].push(currentTime); socket.emit('updateDraftReceived', data); }); next(); }; }; // Apply rate limiter middleware to Socket.IO io.use(EventEmitterRateLimiter(10, 60000)); // Limit to 10 events per minute - Explanation: - Limits each client to emitting updateDraft events a maximum of 10 times per minute. - Sends an error message if the rate limit is exceeded. > Note: Customize maxEvents and windowMs based on your application's requirements. --- ### 7. Comprehensive Example: Enhanced and Secured index.js Combining all the enhancements and security measures discussed, here's how your final index.js might look: // index.js require('dotenv').config(); const express = require('express'); const axios = require('axios'); const rateLimit = require('express-rate-limit'); const winston = require('winston'); const https = require('https'); const { Server } = require('socket.io'); const fs = require('fs'); const path = require('path'); const jwt = require('jsonwebtoken'); const validator = require('validator'); const helmet = require('helmet'); const app = express(); const PORT = process.env.PORT || 3000; // Configure Winston Logger const logger = winston.createLogger({ level: 'info', format: winston.format.combine( winston.format.timestamp(), winston.format.json() ), transports: [ new winston.transports.Console(), new winston.transports.File({ filename: 'error.log', level: 'error' }), new winston.transports.File({ filename: 'combined.log' }), ], }); // Use Helmet for Security Headers app.use(helmet({ contentSecurityPolicy: { directives: { defaultSrc: ["'self'"], scriptSrc: ["'self'", "https://cdn.socket.io"], connectSrc: ["'self'", "https://your-backend-domain.com"], imgSrc: ["'self'", "https://c1.scryfall.com"], styleSrc: ["'self'", "'unsafe-inline'"], // Add other directives as needed }, }, })); // Rate Limiting Middleware for HTTP Requests const httpLimiter = rateLimit({ windowMs: parseInt(process.env.RATE_LIMIT_WINDOW_MS) || 60000, // 1 minute max: parseInt(process.env.RATE_LIMIT_MAX) || 100, // limit each IP to 100 requests per windowMs message: 'Too many requests from this IP, please try again after a minute.', handler: (req, res, next, options) => { logger.warn( Rate limit exceeded for IP: ${req.ip} ); res.status(options.statusCode).json({ message: options.message }); }, }); app.use(httpLimiter); // 17lands API Service const seventeenLandsAPI = axios.create({ baseURL: process.env.SEVENTEENLANDS_API_URL, headers: { 'Authorization': Bearer ${process.env.SEVENTEENLANDS_API_KEY} , 'Content-Type': 'application/json', }, timeout: 5000, // 5 seconds timeout }); // Endpoint to Fetch Win Rates and ALSA Data for a Specific Card app.get('/api/cards/:cardName/stats', async (req, res) => { const { cardName } = req.params; try { const response = await seventeenLandsAPI.get( /cards/${encodeURIComponent(cardName)}/stats ); res.json(response.data); } catch (error) { if (error.response) { // API responded with a status outside 2xx logger.error( 17lands API Error: ${error.response.status} - ${error.response.data} ); res.status(error.response.status).json({ message: error.response.data }); } else if (error.request) { // No response received logger.error( 17lands API No Response: ${error.message} ); res.status(503).json({ message: '17lands API is unavailable. Please try again later.' }); } else { // Other errors logger.error( Error fetching 17lands data: ${error.message} ); res.status(500).json({ message: 'Internal Server Error' }); } } }); // Create HTTPS Server const sslOptions = { key: fs.readFileSync(path.join(__dirname, 'certs', 'key.pem')), cert: fs.readFileSync(path.join(__dirname, 'certs', 'cert.pem')), }; const httpsServer = https.createServer(sslOptions, app); // Initialize Socket.IO Server with HTTPS Server const io = new Server(httpsServer, { cors: { origin: process.env.CLIENT_ORIGIN || '*', // Replace '*' with your frontend domain in production methods: ['GET', 'POST'], }, }); // Redis Adapter for Scaling (Optional) // Uncomment and configure if scaling across multiple instances /* const { createAdapter } = require('@socket.io/redis-adapter'); const { createClient } = require('redis'); const pubClient = createClient({ host: process.env.REDIS_HOST, port: process.env.REDIS_PORT, password: process.env.REDIS_PASSWORD }); const subClient = pubClient.duplicate(); pubClient.connect().catch(console.error); subClient.connect().catch(console.error); io.adapter(createAdapter(pubClient, subClient)); */ // Middleware for authenticating Socket.IO connections io.use((socket, next) => { const token = socket.handshake.auth.token; if (!token) { logger.warn( Socket ${socket.id} missing authentication token. ); return next(new Error('Authentication error')); } jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET, (err, decoded) => { if (err) { logger.warn( Socket ${socket.id} provided invalid token. ); return next(new Error('Authentication error')); } socket.user = decoded; // Attach decoded token data to socket next(); }); }); // Rate Limiting Middleware for Socket.IO Events const EventEmitterRateLimiter = (maxEvents, windowMs) => { const clients = {}; return (socket, next) => { // Example: Rate limit for 'updateDraft' events socket.on('updateDraft', (data) => { const clientId = socket.id; const currentTime = Date.now(); if (!clients[clientId]) { clients[clientId] = []; } // Remove events older than windowMs clients[clientId] = clients[clientId].filter(timestamp => currentTime - timestamp = maxEvents) { logger.warn( Rate limit exceeded for socket ${clientId} ); socket.emit('error', { message: 'Rate limit exceeded. Please slow down.' }); return; } clients[clientId].push(currentTime); // Proceed with handling the event // Emit back to client or handle as needed socket.emit('updateDraftReceived', data); }); next(); }; }; // Apply rate limiter middleware to Socket.IO io.use(EventEmitterRateLimiter(10, 60000)); // Limit to 10 'updateDraft' events per minute // Handle Socket.IO Connections io.on('connection', (socket) => { logger.info( Authenticated client connected: ${socket.id} (User ID: ${socket.user.id}) ); // Event: joinDraft - Join a specific draft room socket.on('joinDraft', (draftId) => { // Validate draftId if (!validator.isAlphanumeric(draftId)) { logger.warn( Invalid draftId provided by socket ${socket.id}: ${draftId} ); return socket.emit('error', { message: 'Invalid draft ID.' }); } // Verify if the user is authorized to join the draft verifyUserDraftAccess(socket.user.id, draftId).then(isAuthorized => { if (!isAuthorized) { logger.warn( Unauthorized draft access attempt by socket ${socket.id} for draft ${draftId} ); return socket.emit('error', { message: 'Unauthorized access to this draft.' }); } socket.join( draft_${draftId} ); logger.info( Socket ${socket.id} joined draft_${draftId} ); socket.emit('joinedDraft', { draftId, message: Joined draft ${draftId} }); }).catch(err => { logger.error( Error verifying draft access for socket ${socket.id}: ${err.message} ); socket.emit('error', { message: 'Internal server error.' }); }); }); // Event: leaveDraft - Leave a specific draft room socket.on('leaveDraft', (draftId) => { if (!validator.isAlphanumeric(draftId)) { logger.warn( Invalid draftId provided by socket ${socket.id}: ${draftId} ); return socket.emit('error', { message: 'Invalid draft ID.' }); } socket.leave( draft_${draftId} ); logger.info( Socket ${socket.id} left draft_${draftId} ); socket.emit('leftDraft', { draftId, message: Left draft ${draftId} }); }); // Event: updateDraft - Receive draft updates from client socket.on('updateDraft', (data) => { const { draftId, update } = data; // Validate draftId if (!validator.isAlphanumeric(draftId)) { logger.warn( Invalid draftId in updateDraft from socket ${socket.id}: ${draftId} ); return socket.emit('error', { message: 'Invalid draft ID.' }); } // Sanitize update data if (typeof update !== 'object' || Array.isArray(update)) { logger.warn( Invalid update data from socket ${socket.id}: ${JSON.stringify(update)} ); return socket.emit('error', { message: 'Invalid update data format.' }); } const sanitizedUpdate = sanitizeObject(update); logger.info( Received update for draft ${draftId} from socket ${socket.id}: ${JSON.stringify(sanitizedUpdate)} ); // Broadcast the update to other clients in the same draft room socket.to( draft_${draftId} ).emit('draftUpdated', { draftId, update: sanitizedUpdate }); }); // Event: sendRecommendations - Receive recommendations from server socket.on('sendRecommendations', (data) => { const { draftId, recommendations } = data; // Validate draftId if (!validator.isAlphanumeric(draftId)) { logger.warn( Invalid draftId in sendRecommendations from socket ${socket.id}: ${draftId} ); return socket.emit('error', { message: 'Invalid draft ID.' }); } // Sanitize recommendations if (!Array.isArray(recommendations)) { logger.warn( Invalid recommendations format from socket ${socket.id}: ${JSON.stringify(recommendations)} ); return socket.emit('error', { message: 'Invalid recommendations format.' }); } const sanitizedRecommendations = recommendations.map(rec => sanitizeObject(rec)); logger.info( Received recommendations for draft ${draftId} from socket ${socket.id}: ${JSON.stringify(sanitizedRecommendations)} ); // Broadcast recommendations to other clients in the same draft room socket.to( draft_${draftId} ).emit('recommendationsReceived', { draftId, recommendations: sanitizedRecommendations }); }); // Handle disconnection socket.on('disconnect', () => { logger.info( Client disconnected: ${socket.id} ); }); }); // Function to Broadcast Updates to a Specific Draft Room const broadcastDraftUpdate = (draftId, updateData) => { const sanitizedData = sanitizeObject(updateData); io.to( draft_${draftId} ).emit('draftUpdate', sanitizedData); logger.info( Broadcasted sanitized update to draft_${draftId} ); }; // Example Endpoint to Trigger a Draft Update (For Testing) app.post('/api/drafts/:id/update', express.json(), (req, res) => { const { id } = req.params; const updateData = req.body; // Expecting JSON data // Validate draftId if (!validator.isAlphanumeric(id)) { return res.status(400).json({ message: 'Invalid draft ID.' }); } // Sanitize updateData const sanitizedData = sanitizeObject(updateData); broadcastDraftUpdate(id, sanitizedData); res.json({ message: 'Draft update broadcasted successfully.' }); }); // Function to Verify User's Access to a Draft async function verifyUserDraftAccess(userId, draftId) { // Implement your logic to verify if the user is part of the draft session // For example, query your database to check draft participation // Return true if authorized, false otherwise // Placeholder implementation: return true; // Assume all users are authorized for demonstration } // Function to Sanitize Objects function sanitizeObject(obj) { const sanitized = {}; for (const key in obj) { if (typeof obj[key] === 'string') { sanitized[key] = validator.escape(obj[key]); } else if (typeof obj[key] === 'object' && obj[key] !== null) { sanitized[key] = sanitizeObject(obj[key]); } else { sanitized[key] = obj[key]; } } return sanitized; } // Start the HTTPS Server httpsServer.listen(PORT, () => { logger.info( 17lands API Service with WebSocket (HTTPS) running on port ${PORT} ); }); Key Points in the Final Example: 1. HTTPS Server Configuration: - Serves Socket.IO over HTTPS using SSL certificates. - Ensures encrypted communication between clients and server. 2. Authentication Middleware: - Verifies JWT tokens during the Socket.IO handshake. - Attaches user information to the socket object for authorization checks. 3. Rate Limiting for WebSocket Events: - Limits the number of specific events (e.g., updateDraft ) a client can emit within a set timeframe. - Prevents abuse and ensures server stability. 4. Data Validation and Sanitization: - Validates and sanitizes all incoming data to prevent injection attacks. - Ensures that only well-formed data is processed and broadcasted. 5. Room Management: - Utilizes rooms ( draft_{draftId} ) to manage different draft sessions. - Ensures that events are broadcasted only to relevant clients. 6. Error Handling: - Emits error events to clients when invalid data is received. - Logs warnings and errors for monitoring and debugging purposes. 7. Scalability: - Prepared for horizontal scaling using Redis Adapter (commented out in the example). - Can handle multiple instances and distribute load effectively. 8. Security Headers: - Uses Helmet to set secure HTTP headers, enhancing the overall security posture. --- ### 8. Testing Your WebSocket Server After implementing the WebSocket server with all the event handlers and security measures, it's crucial to test its functionality to ensure everything works as expected. #### 8.1. Using Socket.IO Client for Testing Example: Socket.IO Test Client Socket.IO Test Client // Replace with your actual JWT token const token = 'your_jwt_token_here'; const socket = io('https://your-backend-domain.com', { auth: { token: token } }); socket.on('connect', () => { console.log( Connected with ID: ${socket.id} ); const draftId = '12345'; // Example draft ID socket.emit('joinDraft', draftId); }); socket.on('joinedDraft', (data) => { console.log(data.message); // Send a draft update const update = { currentPick: 'Black Lotus', pickedBy: 'Player1' }; socket.emit('updateDraft', { draftId: '12345', update: update }); }); socket.on('draftUpdated', (data) => { console.log( Draft Updated for ${data.draftId}: , data.update); }); socket.on('synergyScoresUpdated', (synergyScores) => { console.log('Synergy Scores Updated:', synergyScores); }); socket.on('recommendationsUpdated', (recommendations) => { console.log('Recommendations Updated:', recommendations); }); socket.on('error', (error) => { console.error('Socket.IO Error:', error.message); }); socket.on('disconnect', () => { console.log('Disconnected from server.'); }); Steps to Test: 1. Serve the test_client.html File: - Open the test_client.html file in a browser. - Ensure that the JWT token is valid and corresponds to a user authorized to join the specified draft. 2. Observe the Console Logs: - Check for connection confirmations, joined draft messages, and any broadcasted updates. 3. Trigger Server-Side Events: - Use tools like [Postman](https://www.postman.com/) to send POST requests to /api/drafts/:id/update to simulate server-side updates. - Example: curl -X POST https://your-backend-domain.com/api/drafts/12345/update \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"currentPick": "Sol Ring", "pickedBy": "Player2"}' - Expected Outcome: - The connected client should receive the draftUpdated event with the new pick information. 4. Test Rate Limiting: - Attempt to emit more updateDraft events than allowed within the specified timeframe to ensure rate limiting works. - Expected Outcome: - The client should receive an error message indicating that the rate limit has been exceeded. 5. Test Authentication: - Try connecting with an invalid or missing JWT token to ensure that unauthorized connections are rejected. - Expected Outcome: - The client should receive an authentication error and fail to connect. --- ### 9. Best Practices Summary 1. Use Secure Protocols (WSS): - Always serve Socket.IO over HTTPS to encrypt data in transit. 2. Authenticate and Authorize Clients: - Implement robust authentication (e.g., JWT) to verify client identities. - Authorize clients based on their roles and permissions. 3. Validate and Sanitize All Data: - Prevent injection attacks by validating and sanitizing incoming and outgoing data. 4. Implement Rate Limiting: - Protect your server from abuse by limiting the number of specific events a client can emit. 5. Manage Rooms Effectively: - Use rooms to segment different draft sessions, ensuring that data is broadcasted only to relevant clients. 6. Scale Horizontally with Redis Adapter: - Enable horizontal scaling by using the Redis adapter, allowing multiple server instances to communicate seamlessly. 7. Monitor and Log Activities: - Keep comprehensive logs for monitoring, debugging, and security auditing. - Use monitoring tools to track server performance and event handling. 8. Handle Errors Gracefully: - Ensure that both server and client handle errors without crashing or exposing sensitive information. 9. Regularly Update Dependencies: - Keep all libraries and packages up-to-date to benefit from security patches and new features. 10. Test Thoroughly: - Implement unit tests and integration tests to verify the correctness and robustness of your WebSocket implementation. --- ## Conclusion By following the detailed steps outlined above, you've successfully set up a secure, efficient, and scalable WebSocket server using Socket.IO in your Node.js backend. This setup facilitates real-time communication between your server and frontend clients, enabling features like live draft updates, synergy score broadcasting, and recommendation sharing. ### Next Steps: 1. Implement Frontend Integration: - Develop the frontend components to handle real-time events and update the UI accordingly. - Ensure that the frontend connects securely to the WebSocket server using valid JWT tokens. 2. Enhance Recommendation Logic: - Integrate the recommendation engine with the real-time data streaming to provide dynamic suggestions based on draft progress. 3. Optimize Performance: - Continuously monitor the performance of your WebSocket server and optimize as needed to handle increasing loads. 4. Implement Additional Security Measures: - Consider using Content Security Policies (CSP), secure cookies, and other security best practices to further safeguard your application. 5. Deploy and Monitor: - Deploy your application to a production environment. - Use monitoring and alerting tools to keep track of server health and respond to issues promptly. Feel free to reach out if you need further assistance with the upcoming phases, frontend integration, or any specific feature implementations. Good luck with building your Draft Navigator Overlay App !

Certainly! Continuing with Phase 1: Core Functionality , we'll now focus on 1.4: Create API Endpoints for Draft Data Retrieval . This involves designing RESTful API endpoints, implementing various HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT), and securing these endpoints using JWT-based authentication. --- ## Phase 1.4: Create API Endpoints for Draft Data Retrieval ### Overview In this section, we'll: 1. Design RESTful API Endpoint Structure : Outline the necessary endpoints for managing draft data. 2. Implement GET Endpoint for Current Draft Picks : Retrieve current picks in a draft session. 3. Develop POST Endpoint to Start a New Draft : Initialize and store a new draft session. 4. Create PUT Endpoint to Update Draft Picks : Update draft picks as users make selections. 5. Implement Security for API Endpoints : Secure all endpoints using JWT authentication. --- ### 1. Design RESTful API Endpoint Structure Prompt: > "Outline the RESTful API endpoints needed for draft data retrieval, such as /api/drafts , /api/drafts/:id/picks , and /api/drafts/:id/recommendations ." Implementation Steps: To effectively manage draft data, we'll define the following RESTful API endpoints: 1. Draft Management Endpoints: - Create a New Draft: - Method: POST - Endpoint: /api/drafts - Description: Initializes a new draft session. - Get All Drafts (Optional): - Method: GET - Endpoint: /api/drafts - Description: Retrieves a list of all draft sessions. Useful for admin purposes. 2. Draft-Specific Endpoints: - Get Current Draft Picks: - Method: GET - Endpoint: /api/drafts/:id/picks - Description: Retrieves the current picks in a specific draft session. - Update Draft Picks: - Method: PUT - Endpoint: /api/drafts/:id/picks - Description: Updates the draft picks as users make their selections. - Get Recommendations: - Method: GET - Endpoint: /api/drafts/:id/recommendations - Description: Retrieves recommendations for the current draft session. 3. Additional Endpoints (Optional): - Delete a Draft: - Method: DELETE - Endpoint: /api/drafts/:id - Description: Deletes a specific draft session. - Get Specific Draft Details: - Method: GET - Endpoint: /api/drafts/:id - Description: Retrieves detailed information about a specific draft. Endpoint Structure Summary: | HTTP Method | Endpoint | Description | |-------------|---------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------| | POST | /api/drafts | Initialize a new draft session. | | GET | /api/drafts | Retrieve all draft sessions (admin use). | | GET | /api/drafts/:id/picks | Get current picks in a draft session. | | PUT | /api/drafts/:id/picks | Update draft picks as selections are made. | | GET | /api/drafts/:id/recommendations | Get recommendations for the draft session. | | DELETE | /api/drafts/:id | Delete a specific draft session. | | GET | /api/drafts/:id | Get detailed information about a draft session. | --- ### 2. Implement GET Endpoint for Current Draft Picks Prompt: > "Create a GET /api/drafts/:id/picks endpoint in Express.js that retrieves the current picks in a specific draft session from the PostgreSQL database." Implementation Steps: 1. Define the Sequelize Models (Assuming Not Already Defined): - Draft Model: // models/Draft.js module.exports = (sequelize, DataTypes) => { const Draft = sequelize.define('Draft', { id: { type: DataTypes.UUID, defaultValue: DataTypes.UUIDV4, primaryKey: true, }, status: { type: DataTypes.ENUM('ongoing', 'completed'), defaultValue: 'ongoing', }, createdAt: { type: DataTypes.DATE, allowNull: false, }, updatedAt: { type: DataTypes.DATE, allowNull: false, }, }, { tableName: 'drafts', }); Draft.associate = (models) => { Draft.hasMany(models.DraftedCard, { foreignKey: 'draftId', as: 'picks' }); }; return Draft; }; - DraftedCard Model: // models/DraftedCard.js module.exports = (sequelize, DataTypes) => { const DraftedCard = sequelize.define('DraftedCard', { id: { type: DataTypes.UUID, defaultValue: DataTypes.UUIDV4, primaryKey: true, }, draftId: { type: DataTypes.UUID, allowNull: false, references: { model: 'drafts', key: 'id', }, onDelete: 'CASCADE', }, userId: { type: DataTypes.UUID, allowNull: false, references: { model: 'users', key: 'id', }, onDelete: 'CASCADE', }, cardId: { type: DataTypes.UUID, allowNull: false, references: { model: 'cards', key: 'id', }, onDelete: 'CASCADE', }, pickedAt: { type: DataTypes.DATE, defaultValue: DataTypes.NOW, }, createdAt: { type: DataTypes.DATE, allowNull: false, }, updatedAt: { type: DataTypes.DATE, allowNull: false, }, }, { tableName: 'drafted_cards', }); DraftedCard.associate = (models) => { DraftedCard.belongsTo(models.Draft, { foreignKey: 'draftId', as: 'draft' }); DraftedCard.belongsTo(models.User, { foreignKey: 'userId', as: 'user' }); DraftedCard.belongsTo(models.Card, { foreignKey: 'cardId', as: 'card' }); }; return DraftedCard; }; > Note: Ensure that these models are correctly initialized and synchronized with your PostgreSQL database using Sequelize migrations. 2. Implement the GET /api/drafts/:id/picks Endpoint: Route Definition: We'll use Express.js to define the route. It's recommended to organize routes using Express Router for better modularity. - Create a drafts.js Router: // routes/drafts.js const express = require('express'); const router = express.Router(); const { Draft, DraftedCard, Card, User } = require('../models'); const authenticate = require('../middleware/authenticate'); // JWT authentication middleware const { Op } = require('sequelize'); // GET /api/drafts/:id/picks router.get('/:id/picks', authenticate, async (req, res) => { const { id } = req.params; const userId = req.user.id; // Assuming authenticate middleware attaches user info to req.user try { // Validate draft ID if (!id || !/^[0-9a-fA-F-]{36}$/.test(id)) { // UUID validation return res.status(400).json({ message: 'Invalid draft ID format.' }); } // Check if the draft exists and the user is part of it const draft = await Draft.findOne({ where: { id: id }, include: [{ model: DraftedCard, as: 'picks', include: [ { model: User, as: 'user', attributes: ['id', 'username'] }, { model: Card, as: 'card', attributes: ['id', 'name', 'manaCost', 'typeLine', 'oracleText', 'imageUri'] }, ], }], }); if (!draft) { return res.status(404).json({ message: 'Draft session not found.' }); } // (Optional) Check if the user is part of the draft // Implement your logic here, e.g., check if user has a pick in the draft // Retrieve current picks const picks = draft.picks.map(pick => ({ pickId: pick.id, user: { id: pick.user.id, username: pick.user.username, }, card: { id: pick.card.id, name: pick.card.name, manaCost: pick.card.manaCost, typeLine: pick.card.typeLine, oracleText: pick.card.oracleText, imageUri: pick.card.imageUri, }, pickedAt: pick.pickedAt, })); res.json({ draftId: id, picks: picks }); } catch (error) { console.error( Error fetching draft picks for draft ${id}: , error); res.status(500).json({ message: 'Internal Server Error' }); } }); module.exports = router; - Integrate the Router in index.js : // index.js (continued) const draftsRouter = require('./routes/drafts'); // Use JSON middleware for parsing request bodies app.use(express.json()); // Mount the drafts router at /api/drafts app.use('/api/drafts', draftsRouter); // ... existing code ... - Authentication Middleware: Implement JWT authentication middleware to protect the endpoint. // middleware/authenticate.js const jwt = require('jsonwebtoken'); const winston = require('winston'); module.exports = (req, res, next) => { const authHeader = req.headers.authorization; if (!authHeader || !authHeader.startsWith('Bearer ')) { return res.status(401).json({ message: 'Authorization header missing or malformed.' }); } const token = authHeader.split(' ')[1]; jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET, (err, decoded) => { if (err) { winston.warn( Invalid JWT token: ${err.message} ); return res.status(401).json({ message: 'Invalid or expired token.' }); } req.user = decoded; // Attach decoded token data to request next(); }); }; 3. Testing the Endpoint: Use tools like [Postman](https://www.postman.com/) or curl to test the endpoint. - Example Request: curl -H "Authorization: Bearer your_jwt_token_here" https://your-backend-domain.com/api/drafts/123e4567-e89b-12d3-a456-426614174000/picks - Expected Response: { "draftId": "123e4567-e89b-12d3-a456-426614174000", "picks": [ { "pickId": "pick-uuid-1", "user": { "id": "user-uuid-1", "username": "PlayerOne" }, "card": { "id": "card-uuid-1", "name": "Black Lotus", "manaCost": "{0}", "typeLine": "Artifact", "oracleText": "{T}, Sacrifice Black Lotus: Add three mana of any one color.", "imageUri": "https://c1.scryfall.com/file/scryfall-cards/normal/front/5/3/534ed3e0-f8b6-4bf4-86f6-38fa6059e8de.jpg?1687522176" }, "pickedAt": "2024-04-27T12:00:00.000Z" }, // ... other picks ... ] } - Error Responses: - 401 Unauthorized: Missing or invalid JWT token. - 400 Bad Request: Invalid draft ID format. - 404 Not Found: Draft session not found. - 500 Internal Server Error: Server-side issues. --- ### 3. Develop POST Endpoint to Start a New Draft Prompt: > "Implement a POST /api/drafts endpoint that initializes a new draft session, assigns a unique draft ID, and stores initial draft data in the database." Implementation Steps: 1. Define the Endpoint in drafts.js Router: Route Definition: // routes/drafts.js (continued) // POST /api/drafts router.post('/', authenticate, async (req, res) => { const userId = req.user.id; // Assuming authenticate middleware attaches user info to req.user const { participants } = req.body; // Expecting an array of user IDs participating in the draft // Validate participants if (!Array.isArray(participants) || participants.length { return DraftParticipant.create({ draftId: newDraft.id, userId: participantId, }); })); res.status(201).json({ message: 'Draft session created successfully.', draftId: newDraft.id }); } catch (error) { console.error('Error creating new draft session:', error); res.status(500).json({ message: 'Internal Server Error' }); } }); module.exports = router; 2. Define the DraftParticipant Model (If Not Already Defined): DraftParticipant Model: // models/DraftParticipant.js module.exports = (sequelize, DataTypes) => { const DraftParticipant = sequelize.define('DraftParticipant', { id: { type: DataTypes.UUID, defaultValue: DataTypes.UUIDV4, primaryKey: true, }, draftId: { type: DataTypes.UUID, allowNull: false, references: { model: 'drafts', key: 'id', }, onDelete: 'CASCADE', }, userId: { type: DataTypes.UUID, allowNull: false, references: { model: 'users', key: 'id', }, onDelete: 'CASCADE', }, createdAt: { type: DataTypes.DATE, allowNull: false, }, updatedAt: { type: DataTypes.DATE, allowNull: false, }, }, { tableName: 'draft_participants', }); DraftParticipant.associate = (models) => { DraftParticipant.belongsTo(models.Draft, { foreignKey: 'draftId', as: 'draft' }); DraftParticipant.belongsTo(models.User, { foreignKey: 'userId', as: 'user' }); }; return DraftParticipant; }; - Associations: - A Draft can have multiple DraftParticipants . - A User can participate in multiple Drafts through DraftParticipants . > Note: If your application handles draft participants differently, adjust the model accordingly. 3. Implement Validations and Error Handling: - Ensure Participants Exist: - Before creating DraftParticipant entries, verify that each user ID in the participants array exists in the users table. - Example Enhancement: // routes/drafts.js (continued) const { User, DraftParticipant } = require('../models'); // POST /api/drafts router.post('/', authenticate, async (req, res) => { const userId = req.user.id; const { participants } = req.body; if (!Array.isArray(participants) || participants.length { return DraftParticipant.create({ draftId: newDraft.id, userId: participantId, }); })); res.status(201).json({ message: 'Draft session created successfully.', draftId: newDraft.id }); } catch (error) { console.error('Error creating new draft session:', error); res.status(500).json({ message: 'Internal Server Error' }); } }); 4. Testing the Endpoint: - Example Request: curl -X POST https://your-backend-domain.com/api/drafts \ -H "Authorization: Bearer your_jwt_token_here" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{ "participants": [ "user-uuid-1", "user-uuid-2", "user-uuid-3" ] }' - Expected Response: { "message": "Draft session created successfully.", "draftId": "123e4567-e89b-12d3-a456-426614174000" } - Error Responses: - 400 Bad Request: Invalid participants array or non-existent user IDs. - 401 Unauthorized: Missing or invalid JWT token. - 500 Internal Server Error: Server-side issues. --- ### 4. Create PUT Endpoint to Update Draft Picks Prompt: > "Develop a PUT /api/drafts/:id/picks endpoint that updates the draft picks as users make their selections. Ensure data validation and update the database accordingly." Implementation Steps: 1. Define the Endpoint in drafts.js Router: Route Definition: // routes/drafts.js (continued) // PUT /api/drafts/:id/picks router.put('/:id/picks', authenticate, async (req, res) => { const { id } = req.params; const userId = req.user.id; const { cardId } = req.body; // Expecting the ID of the card being picked // Validate input if (!cardId || !/^[0-9a-fA-F-]{36}$/.test(cardId)) { // UUID validation return res.status(400).json({ message: 'Invalid or missing card ID.' }); } try { // Validate draft ID if (!id || !/^[0-9a-fA-F-]{36}$/.test(id)) { return res.status(400).json({ message: 'Invalid draft ID format.' }); } // Check if the draft exists and is ongoing const draft = await Draft.findOne({ where: { id: id, status: 'ongoing' }, include: [{ model: DraftParticipant, as: 'participants', where: { userId: userId }, attributes: [], // No need to retrieve participant data }], }); if (!draft) { return res.status(404).json({ message: 'Draft session not found or already completed.' }); } // Check if the user has already made a pick (prevent duplicate picks) const existingPick = await DraftedCard.findOne({ where: { draftId: id, userId: userId }, }); if (existingPick) { return res.status(400).json({ message: 'User has already made a pick in this draft.' }); } // Verify that the card exists const card = await Card.findOne({ where: { id: cardId } }); if (!card) { return res.status(400).json({ message: 'Card not found.' }); } // Create a new draft pick const newPick = await DraftedCard.create({ draftId: id, userId: userId, cardId: cardId, }); res.status(200).json({ message: 'Draft pick updated successfully.', pick: { pickId: newPick.id, userId: userId, cardId: cardId, pickedAt: newPick.pickedAt, }, }); } catch (error) { console.error( Error updating draft picks for draft ${id}: , error); res.status(500).json({ message: 'Internal Server Error' }); } }); module.exports = router; 2. Implement Real-Time Updates via Socket.IO (Optional): To ensure that all participants are notified of new picks in real-time, integrate Socket.IO broadcasting within this endpoint. // routes/drafts.js (continued) const io = require('../index').io; // Assuming Socket.IO instance is exported from index.js // Inside the PUT /api/drafts/:id/picks endpoint, after creating a new pick: // Broadcast the new pick to all clients in the draft room io.to( draft_${id} ).emit('newPick', { pickId: newPick.id, userId: userId, cardId: cardId, pickedAt: newPick.pickedAt, }); > Note: Ensure that the Socket.IO instance ( io ) is properly exported from index.js to be accessible in other modules. 3. Implement Additional Validations (Optional): - Check if the Card is Available: - Ensure that the card hasn't been drafted already if each card is unique per draft. - Manage Draft Turns: - Implement logic to ensure that users make picks in a specific order or within their turn. 4. Implement Security for API Endpoints Prompt: > "Add authentication middleware to secure your API endpoints. Use JWT tokens to verify user identity and protect draft data from unauthorized access." Implementation Steps: 1. Ensure JWT Authentication Middleware is Implemented: - As previously defined in the GET Endpoint and POST Endpoint , the authenticate middleware is used to protect routes. - Review the Middleware: // middleware/authenticate.js const jwt = require('jsonwebtoken'); const winston = require('winston'); module.exports = (req, res, next) => { const authHeader = req.headers.authorization; if (!authHeader || !authHeader.startsWith('Bearer ')) { return res.status(401).json({ message: 'Authorization header missing or malformed.' }); } const token = authHeader.split(' ')[1]; jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET, (err, decoded) => { if (err) { winston.warn( Invalid JWT token: ${err.message} ); return res.status(401).json({ message: 'Invalid or expired token.' }); } req.user = decoded; // Attach decoded token data to request next(); }); }; 2. Apply Authentication Middleware to All Protected Routes: - In routes/drafts.js , ensure that all routes are protected by the authenticate middleware. - Example: // routes/drafts.js (continued) const express = require('express'); const router = express.Router(); const { Draft, DraftedCard, Card, User, DraftParticipant } = require('../models'); const authenticate = require('../middleware/authenticate'); const { Op } = require('sequelize'); const io = require('../index').io; // Socket.IO instance const validator = require('validator'); // Apply authenticate middleware to all routes in this router router.use(authenticate); // ... existing route definitions ... - Note: Alternatively, apply the middleware individually to each route as shown in previous examples. 3. Enhance Security with Additional Measures: - Role-Based Access Control (RBAC): - Assign roles (e.g., admin, user) to users and restrict access to certain endpoints based on roles. - Example: // middleware/authorize.js module.exports = (roles = []) => { // roles param can be a single role string (e.g., 'admin') or an array of roles if (typeof roles === 'string') { roles = [roles]; } return [ (req, res, next) => { if (!roles.length || roles.includes(req.user.role)) { return next(); } res.status(403).json({ message: 'Forbidden: You do not have access to this resource.' }); } ]; }; - Usage: // routes/drafts.js (continued) const authorize = require('../middleware/authorize'); // Example: Only admins can retrieve all drafts router.get('/', authorize('admin'), async (req, res) => { // Implementation... }); - Input Validation and Sanitization: - Ensure all incoming data is validated and sanitized to prevent injection attacks. - Utilize libraries like validator.js for robust validation. - Implement HTTPS Strict Transport Security (HSTS): - Enforce HTTPS connections to enhance security. - Already partially handled by serving over HTTPS and using Helmet. - Use Secure Cookies: - If your application uses cookies (e.g., for session management), ensure they are marked as Secure , HttpOnly , and use the SameSite attribute. - Example (Using Express Sessions): const session = require('express-session'); app.use(session({ secret: process.env.SESSION_SECRET, resave: false, saveUninitialized: false, cookie: { secure: true, // Ensures the browser only sends the cookie over HTTPS httpOnly: true, // Prevents client-side JavaScript from accessing the cookie sameSite: 'strict', // Protects against CSRF }, })); > Note: Ensure that secure: true is only set in production environments where HTTPS is enforced. 4. Implement Rate Limiting for API Endpoints: - Already partially implemented in the GET and POST routes. - Example: // routes/drafts.js (continued) // Apply rate limiter to specific routes if needed const specificLimiter = rateLimit({ windowMs: 60 * 1000, // 1 minute max: 30, // limit each IP to 30 requests per windowMs message: 'Too many requests, please try again later.', }); // Apply to the GET /api/drafts/:id/picks endpoint router.get('/:id/picks', specificLimiter, async (req, res) => { // Implementation... }); 5. Use Helmet to Set Secure HTTP Headers: - Already implemented in the GET Endpoint section to set CSP and other security headers. - Example: // index.js (continued) const helmet = require('helmet'); app.use(helmet({ contentSecurityPolicy: { directives: { defaultSrc: ["'self'"], scriptSrc: ["'self'", "https://cdn.socket.io"], connectSrc: ["'self'", "https://your-backend-domain.com"], imgSrc: ["'self'", "https://c1.scryfall.com"], styleSrc: ["'self'", "'unsafe-inline'"], // Add other directives as needed }, }, })); --- ### 6. Authentication and Authorization: - All routes are protected by the authenticate middleware. - Admin-specific routes (e.g., retrieving all drafts, deleting a draft) are further protected by the authorize('admin') middleware. 2. Route Implementations: - POST /api/drafts : Initializes a new draft session with specified participants. - GET /api/drafts/:id/picks : Retrieves current picks in a draft session. - PUT /api/drafts/:id/picks : Updates draft picks as users make selections. - GET /api/drafts/:id/recommendations : Retrieves recommendations for the draft session. - GET /api/drafts : Retrieves all draft sessions (admin use). - GET /api/drafts/:id : Retrieves detailed information about a specific draft session. - DELETE /api/drafts/:id : Deletes a specific draft session (admin use). 3. Error Handling: - Comprehensive error handling ensures that clients receive meaningful error messages. - Logs all errors using Winston for monitoring and debugging. 4. Data Validation: - Validates UUID formats for draftId and cardId . - Ensures that input data (e.g., participants array) adheres to expected formats. - Prevents duplicate picks by checking existing entries. 5. Real-Time Broadcasting: - Integrates with Socket.IO to broadcast real-time updates (e.g., new picks) to all clients in a draft room. 6. Security Enhancements: - Validates and sanitizes all incoming data. - Restricts access to admin-specific endpoints using RBAC. --- ### 7. Implementing RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) Purpose: > Restrict certain API endpoints to users with specific roles (e.g., admin). Implementation Steps: 1. Define User Roles: - Ensure that the User model has a role attribute. // models/User.js (continued) module.exports = (sequelize, DataTypes) => { const User = sequelize.define('User', { id: { type: DataTypes.UUID, defaultValue: DataTypes.UUIDV4, primaryKey: true, }, username: { type: DataTypes.STRING, allowNull: false, unique: true, }, email: { type: DataTypes.STRING, allowNull: false, unique: true, validate: { isEmail: true, }, }, passwordHash: { type: DataTypes.STRING, allowNull: false, }, role: { type: DataTypes.ENUM('user', 'admin'), defaultValue: 'user', }, createdAt: { type: DataTypes.DATE, allowNull: false, }, updatedAt: { type: DataTypes.DATE, allowNull: false, }, }, { tableName: 'users', }); User.associate = (models) => { User.hasMany(models.DraftedCard, { foreignKey: 'userId', as: 'picks' }); User.hasMany(models.DraftParticipant, { foreignKey: 'userId', as: 'draftsParticipated' }); }; return User; }; 2. Authorization Middleware: - As previously defined in the Router Example , the authorize middleware restricts access based on user roles. // middleware/authorize.js module.exports = (roles = []) => { // roles param can be a single role string (e.g., 'admin') or an array of roles if (typeof roles === 'string') { roles = [roles]; } return [ (req, res, next) => { if (!roles.length || roles.includes(req.user.role)) { return next(); } res.status(403).json({ message: 'Forbidden: You do not have access to this resource.' }); } ]; }; 3. Applying Authorization Middleware: - Apply the authorize middleware to routes that require specific roles. Example: // routes/drafts.js (continued) const authorize = require('../middleware/authorize'); // DELETE /api/drafts/:id (Admin only) router.delete('/:id', authorize('admin'), async (req, res) => { // Implementation... }); // GET /api/drafts (Admin only) router.get('/', authorize('admin'), async (req, res) => { // Implementation... }); 4. Testing RBAC: - Attempt to access admin-only endpoints with users having different roles. - Ensure that: - Admin users can access admin routes. - Regular users receive a 403 Forbidden response when attempting to access restricted routes. --- ### 8.
