if (!draft) { return res.status(404).json({ message: 'Draft session not found.' }); } // Format draft details const draftDetails = { draftId: draft.id, status: draft.status, createdAt: draft.createdAt, updatedAt: draft.updatedAt, picks: draft.picks.map(pick => ({ pickId: pick.id, user: { id: pick.user.id, username: pick.user.username, }, card: { id: pick.card.id, name: pick.card.name, manaCost: pick.card.manaCost, typeLine: pick.card.typeLine, oracleText: pick.card.oracleText, imageUri: pick.card.imageUri, }, pickedAt: pick.pickedAt, })), }; res.json(draftDetails); } catch (error) { winston.error( Error fetching draft details for draft ${id}: ${error.message} ); res.status(500).json({ message: 'Internal Server Error' }); } }); /** * DELETE /api/drafts/:id * Delete a specific draft session (Admin Use) */ router.delete('/:id', authorize('admin'), async (req, res) => { const { id } = req.params; try { // Validate draft ID format if (!id || !validator.isUUID(id)) { return res.status(400).json({ message: 'Invalid draft ID format.' }); } // Delete the draft const deletedCount = await Draft.destroy({ where: { id: id } }); if (deletedCount === 0) { return res.status(404).json({ message: 'Draft session not found.' }); } res.json({ message: 'Draft session deleted successfully.' }); } catch (error) { winston.error( Error deleting draft ${id}: ${error.message} ); res.status(500).json({ message: 'Internal Server Error' }); } }); module.exports = router; Key Highlights: 1. Security: - All routes are protected by the authenticate middleware to ensure that only authenticated users can access them. - Admin-specific routes ( GET /api/drafts , DELETE /api/drafts/:id ) are further protected by the authorize('admin') middleware to restrict access to administrators. 2. Data Validation and Sanitization: - Validates UUID formats for draftId and cardId using validator.isUUID . - Ensures that input data (e.g., participants array) meets expected formats and constraints. 3. Error Handling: - Comprehensive error handling ensures that clients receive meaningful feedback. - Logs all errors using Winston for monitoring and debugging. 4. Real-Time Updates: - Integrates with Socket.IO to broadcast new picks to all clients in a draft session. - Ensures that all participants receive updates in real-time without needing to refresh. 5. Recommendations Endpoint: - Assumes that recommendations are stored in a Recommendation model. - Retrieves and formats top recommendations based on predefined criteria (e.g., synergy scores). 6. Admin Capabilities: - Admins can retrieve all draft sessions and delete specific drafts, enabling effective management and oversight. --- ### 9. Testing All API Endpoints Best Practices: 1. Best Practices for API Endpoint Development 1. Consistent Naming Conventions: - Use clear and consistent naming for endpoints and HTTP methods following RESTful principles. 2. Use HTTP Status Codes Appropriately: - 200 OK for successful GET, PUT requests. - 201 Created for successful POST requests. - 400 Bad Request for invalid inputs. - 401 Unauthorized for missing or invalid authentication. - 403 Forbidden for unauthorized access based on roles. - 404 Not Found for non-existent resources. - 500 Internal Server Error for unexpected server-side issues. 3. Implement Pagination for Endpoints Returning Large Data Sets: - For endpoints like GET /api/drafts , implement pagination to handle large numbers of drafts efficiently. Example: // Modify GET /api/drafts to include pagination router.get('/', authorize('admin'), async (req, res) => { const { page = 1, limit = 10 } = req.query; const offset = (page - 1) * limit; try { const { rows: drafts, count } = await Draft.findAndCountAll({ include: [{ model: DraftParticipant, as: 'participants', include: [{ model: User, as: 'user', attributes: ['id', 'username'] }], }, { model: DraftedCard, as: 'picks', include: [ { model: User, as: 'user', attributes: ['id', 'username'] }, { model: Card, as: 'card', attributes: ['id', 'name'] }, ], }], order: [['createdAt', 'DESC']], limit: parseInt(limit), offset: parseInt(offset), }); const formattedDrafts = drafts.map(draft => ({ draftId: draft.id, status: draft.status, createdAt: draft.createdAt, updatedAt: draft.updatedAt, participants: draft.participants.map(participant => ({ userId: participant.user.id, username: participant.user.username, })), picks: draft.picks.map(pick => ({ pickId: pick.id, userId: pick.user.id, username: pick.user.username, cardId: pick.card.id, cardName: pick.card.name, pickedAt: pick.pickedAt, })), })); res.json({ totalDrafts: count, currentPage: parseInt(page), totalPages: Math.ceil(count / limit), drafts: formattedDrafts, }); } catch (error) { winston.error( Error fetching all drafts: ${error.message} ); res.status(500).json({ message: 'Internal Server Error' }); } }); 4. Document Your API: - Use tools like [Swagger](https://swagger.io/) or [Postman Documentation](https://learning.postman.com/docs/publishing-your-api/documenting-your-api/) to create comprehensive API documentation. - Ensure that all endpoints, parameters, request/response formats, and error messages are well-documented. 5. Implement Rate Limiting: - Protect your API from abuse by limiting the number of requests a client can make within a specific timeframe. - Already implemented using express-rate-limit in earlier steps. 6. Use HTTPS Exclusively: - Ensure that all API traffic is encrypted by enforcing HTTPS. - Already handled by serving the application over HTTPS. 7. Handle Database Transactions Carefully: - Use transactions for operations that involve multiple database actions to maintain data integrity. Example: const { sequelize } = require('../models'); router.post('/', async (req, res) => { const t = await sequelize.transaction(); try { // Database operations within the transaction const newDraft = await Draft.create({}, { transaction: t }); await DraftParticipant.bulkCreate(participants.map(id => ({ draftId: newDraft.id, userId: id, })), { transaction: t }); await t.commit(); res.status(201).json({ message: 'Draft created.', draftId: newDraft.id }); } catch (error) { await t.rollback(); res.status(500).json({ message: 'Internal Server Error' }); } }); 8. Implement Caching for Frequently Accessed Data: - Use caching mechanisms like Redis to store and retrieve frequently accessed data, reducing database load and improving response times. - Implemented in previous phases. 9. Version Your API: - Include versioning in your API endpoints to manage changes over time without breaking existing clients. Example: // Mount routers with versioning app.use('/api/v1/drafts', draftsRouter); --- ### 11. 1.5 Implement Data Synchronization with MTG Arena Logs Analyze MTG Arena Log Structure "Examine the structure and format of MTG Arena logs to identify how draft information is recorded. Determine the key data points needed for real-time draft synchronization." Implement Real-Time Log Monitoring "Use file-watching libraries like watchdog in Python to monitor MTG Arena log files in real-time. Trigger the log parser whenever new data is appended to the logs." Store Parsed Data in PostgreSQL "Modify your log parser to insert the extracted draft data into the PostgreSQL database. Ensure that data is accurately mapped to the corresponding database tables with proper relationships." Update Backend Services with Parsed Data "Develop backend logic that listens for new draft data in the database and updates the real-time WebSocket clients with the latest information using Socket.IO."

Certainly! Implementing Phase 1.5: Data Synchronization with MTG Arena Logs is a critical step in ensuring that your Draft Navigator Overlay App remains up-to-date with the real-time actions occurring within MTG Arena. This phase involves analyzing MTG Arena's log structure, creating a Python-based log parser, setting up real-time log monitoring, storing parsed data into PostgreSQL, and ensuring that your backend services communicate these updates effectively to your frontend clients via WebSockets. Below is a step-by-step guide to help you accomplish each of these tasks. --- ## Phase 1.5: Implement Data Synchronization with MTG Arena Logs ### Overview 1. Analyze MTG Arena Log Structure 2. Develop Log Parser in Python 3. Implement Real-Time Log Monitoring 4. Store Parsed Data in PostgreSQL 5. Update Backend Services with Parsed Data --- ### 1. Analyze MTG Arena Log Structure Objective: Understand how MTG Arena logs draft-related information to effectively parse and extract necessary data. #### 1.1. Locate MTG Arena Logs MTG Arena maintains several log files that record various in-game events. Typically, these logs can be found in the following directory based on your operating system: - Windows: C:\Users\ \AppData\Local\Wizards Of The Coast\MTGA\Logs - macOS: ~/Library/Logs/Wizards Of The Coast/MTGA/ - Linux: ~/.wine/drive_c/users/ /Local Settings/Application Data/Wizards Of The Coast/MTGA/Logs Note: Replace with your actual system username. #### 1.2. Identify Relevant Log Files Within the Logs directory, you’ll find multiple log files. The primary files of interest for draft synchronization are: - Player.log : Contains player actions, including draft picks. - Analytics.log : Records analytics data which might include draft progress. - Session.log : General session information. For Draft Synchronization: Focus primarily on Player.log as it records detailed player actions during a draft. #### 1.3. Understand Log Format MTG Arena logs are typically in plain text with each line representing a distinct event or action in JSON format or structured log entries. Here's an example of a Player.log entry related to a draft pick: [2024-04-27 12:00:00.000] [Player] [INFO] DraftPick: {"draftId":"123e4567-e89b-12d3-a456-426614174000","userId":"user-uuid-1","cardId":"card-uuid-1","timestamp":"2024-04-27T12:00:00Z"} Key Data Points: - draftId : Unique identifier for the draft session. - userId : Unique identifier for the user making the pick. - cardId : Unique identifier for the card being picked. - timestamp : Time when the pick was made. Other Relevant Entries: - Draft Initialization : Marks the start of a draft session. - Draft Completion : Marks the end of a draft session. - Card Rejections : When a user rejects a card instead of picking. - Player Joining/Leaving : When users join or leave the draft. Understanding these entries is crucial for accurately parsing and synchronizing draft data. --- ### 2. Develop Log Parser in Python Objective: Create a Python script that reads Player.log , parses relevant draft information, and extracts data such as drafted cards, player actions, and draft progress. #### 2.1. Setup the Python Environment 1. Install Required Libraries: We'll use the following Python libraries: - watchdog : For monitoring file changes in real-time. - psycopg2-binary : For interacting with PostgreSQL. - json : For parsing JSON log entries. - re : For regex operations if needed. Installation Command: pip install watchdog psycopg2-binary 2. Project Structure: mtg_log_parser/ ├── parser.py ├── config.py └── requirements.txt #### 2.2. Configuration File ( config.py ) Create a config.py to store configuration details like log file paths and database credentials. # config.py import os # Path to the Player.log file LOG_FILE_PATH = os.path.expanduser('~/AppData/Local/Wizards Of The Coast/MTGA/Logs/Player.log') # Update based on OS # PostgreSQL Database Configuration DB_CONFIG = { 'dbname': 'mtg_draft', 'user': 'your_db_username', 'password': 'your_db_password', 'host': 'localhost', 'port': '5432', } Note: Replace 'your_db_username' and 'your_db_password' with your actual PostgreSQL credentials. Also, adjust the LOG_FILE_PATH based on your operating system. #### 2.3. Database Schema Ensure that your PostgreSQL database has the necessary tables to store draft data. Here's an example schema: -- drafts table CREATE TABLE drafts ( id UUID PRIMARY KEY, status VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'ongoing', created_at TIMESTAMP WITHOUT TIME ZONE DEFAULT (NOW() AT TIME ZONE 'utc'), updated_at TIMESTAMP WITHOUT TIME ZONE DEFAULT (NOW() AT TIME ZONE 'utc') ); -- drafted_cards table CREATE TABLE drafted_cards ( id UUID PRIMARY KEY, draft_id UUID REFERENCES drafts(id) ON DELETE CASCADE, user_id UUID NOT NULL, card_id UUID NOT NULL, picked_at TIMESTAMP WITHOUT TIME ZONE DEFAULT (NOW() AT TIME ZONE 'utc') ); -- recommendations table (optional, if storing recommendations) CREATE TABLE recommendations ( id UUID PRIMARY KEY, draft_id UUID REFERENCES drafts(id) ON DELETE CASCADE, card_id UUID REFERENCES cards(id) ON DELETE CASCADE, score INTEGER NOT NULL, created_at TIMESTAMP WITHOUT TIME ZONE DEFAULT (NOW() AT TIME ZONE 'utc') ); -- cards table (assuming it's pre-populated with all MTG cards) CREATE TABLE cards ( id UUID PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL, mana_cost VARCHAR(50), type_line VARCHAR(255), oracle_text TEXT, image_uri TEXT ); Note: - Ensure that the cards table is populated with all relevant MTG cards. - The recommendations table is optional and depends on whether you plan to store recommendation data. - Adjust the schema based on your specific requirements. #### 2.4. Log Parser Script ( parser.py ) Below is a comprehensive Python script that: - Monitors the Player.log file in real-time. - Parses relevant draft-related log entries. - Inserts parsed data into the PostgreSQL database. - Handles reconnection and error scenarios gracefully. # parser.py import time import json import re import psycopg2 from watchdog.observers import Observer from watchdog.events import FileSystemEventHandler from config import LOG_FILE_PATH, DB_CONFIG import logging from uuid import UUID # Setup logging logging.basicConfig( level=logging.INFO, format='%(asctime)s [%(levelname)s] %(message)s', handlers=[ logging.FileHandler("parser.log"), logging.StreamHandler() ] ) logger = logging.getLogger(__name__) # Helper function to validate UUID def is_valid_uuid(val): try: UUID(str(val)) return True except ValueError: return False # Database connection handler class Database: def __init__(self, config): self.config = config self.conn = None self.connect() def connect(self): try: self.conn = psycopg2.connect(**self.config) self.conn.autocommit = True logger.info("Connected to PostgreSQL database.") except Exception as e: logger.error(f"Failed to connect to PostgreSQL: {e}") self.conn = None def reconnect(self): if self.conn: self.conn.close() self.connect() def insert_draft(self, draft_id): if not is_valid_uuid(draft_id): logger.warning(f"Attempted to insert invalid draft_id: {draft_id}") return try: with self.conn.cursor() as cur: cur.execute( """ INSERT INTO drafts (id, status) VALUES (%s, %s) ON CONFLICT (id) DO NOTHING; """, (draft_id, 'ongoing') ) logger.info(f"Inserted draft: {draft_id}") except Exception as e: logger.error(f"Error inserting draft {draft_id}: {e}") def insert_drafted_card(self, draft_id, user_id, card_id, picked_at): if not all(is_valid_uuid(val) for val in [draft_id, user_id, card_id]): logger.warning(f"Invalid UUID in drafted_card: draft_id={draft_id}, user_id={user_id}, card_id={card_id}") return try: with self.conn.cursor() as cur: cur.execute( """ INSERT INTO drafted_cards (id, draft_id, user_id, card_id, picked_at) VALUES (gen_random_uuid(), %s, %s, %s, %s) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING; """, (draft_id, user_id, card_id, picked_at) ) logger.info(f"Inserted drafted card: draft_id={draft_id}, user_id={user_id}, card_id={card_id}") except Exception as e: logger.error(f"Error inserting drafted card: {e}") def insert_recommendation(self, draft_id, card_id, score): if not all(is_valid_uuid(val) for val in [draft_id, card_id]): logger.warning(f"Invalid UUID in recommendation: draft_id={draft_id}, card_id={card_id}") return try: with self.conn.cursor() as cur: cur.execute( """ INSERT INTO recommendations (id, draft_id, card_id, score) VALUES (gen_random_uuid(), %s, %s, %s) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING; """, (draft_id, card_id, score) ) logger.info(f"Inserted recommendation: draft_id={draft_id}, card_id={card_id}, score={score}") except Exception as e: logger.error(f"Error inserting recommendation: {e}") def close(self): if self.conn: self.conn.close() logger.info("Closed PostgreSQL connection.") # Log Event Handler class LogHandler(FileSystemEventHandler): def __init__(self, db): super().__init__() self.db = db self._file = open(LOG_FILE_PATH, 'r', encoding='utf-8') self._file.seek(0, 2) # Move to the end of the file def on_modified(self, event): if event.src_path != LOG_FILE_PATH: return for line in self._file: self.process_line(line.strip()) def process_line(self, line): # Example log line: # [2024-04-27 12:00:00.000] [Player] [INFO] DraftPick: {"draftId":"123e4567-e89b-12d3-a456-426614174000","userId":"user-uuid-1","cardId":"card-uuid-1","timestamp":"2024-04-27T12:00:00Z"} # Check if the line contains 'DraftPick' if 'DraftPick' not in line: return try: # Extract JSON part using regex json_part = re.search(r'DraftPick:\s*(\{.*\})', line) if not json_part: logger.warning(f"No JSON data found in line: {line}") return data = json.loads(json_part.group(1)) draft_id = data.get('draftId') user_id = data.get('userId') card_id = data.get('cardId') timestamp = data.get('timestamp') # ISO format if not all([draft_id, user_id, card_id, timestamp]): logger.warning(f"Incomplete data in line: {line}") return # Insert draft if not exists self.db.insert_draft(draft_id) # Insert drafted card self.db.insert_drafted_card(draft_id, user_id, card_id, timestamp) # (Optional) Generate and insert recommendations # This depends on your recommendation engine implementation # For demonstration, let's assume a dummy score score = 90 # Replace with actual scoring logic self.db.insert_recommendation(draft_id, card_id, score) # (Optional) Trigger backend update via API or message queue # This depends on your architecture except json.JSONDecodeError as e: logger.error(f"JSON decode error for line: {line} | Error: {e}") except Exception as e: logger.error(f"Error processing line: {line} | Error: {e}") # Main Function def main(): db = Database(DB_CONFIG) event_handler = LogHandler(db) observer = Observer() log_dir = re.sub(r'[^\\/]+$', '', LOG_FILE_PATH) # Get directory from log file path observer.schedule(event_handler, path=log_dir, recursive=False) observer.start() logger.info("Started log observer.") try: while True: time.sleep(1) except KeyboardInterrupt: observer.stop() logger.info("Stopping log observer.") except Exception as e: logger.error(f"Unexpected error: {e}") observer.stop() observer.join() db.close() if __name__ == "__main__": main() #### 2.5. Explanation of the Parser Script 1. Logging Setup: - Configures logging to output both to a file ( parser.log ) and the console for real-time monitoring. 2. Database Connection: - Establishes a connection to the PostgreSQL database using credentials from config.py . - Implements reconnection logic in case of connection failures. 3. Log Monitoring: - Uses watchdog to monitor the Player.log file for any modifications. - Upon detecting a modification, it reads new lines appended to the log. 4. Log Parsing: - Identifies lines containing DraftPick . - Extracts the JSON payload from the log line using regex. - Parses the JSON to extract draftId , userId , cardId , and timestamp . - Validates UUIDs to ensure data integrity. 5. Data Insertion: - Inserts the draft into the drafts table if it doesn't already exist. - Inserts the drafted card into the drafted_cards table. - Optionally, inserts recommendations into the recommendations table (this assumes that recommendations are generated externally or are precomputed). 6. Error Handling: - Logs any JSON decoding errors or unexpected issues during parsing. - Ensures that the parser continues running even if it encounters problematic log lines. 7. Graceful Shutdown: - Listens for keyboard interrupts to gracefully stop the observer and close the database connection. #### 2.6. Enhancements and Best Practices 1. Recommendation Engine Integration: - The current parser inserts a dummy score for recommendations. Integrate your recommendation engine to compute actual synergy scores based on the current draft state. 2. Idempotency: - Ensure that repeated parsing of the same log lines does not result in duplicate entries. This is handled using ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING in SQL insertions. 3. Scalability: - For high-volume drafts, consider batching database insertions to optimize performance. 4. Security: - Secure database credentials, possibly using environment variables or a secrets manager instead of hardcoding them in config.py . 5. Testing: - Create sample log lines and test the parser to ensure accurate data extraction and insertion. 6. Notification Mechanism: - Depending on your architecture, consider using message queues (like RabbitMQ or Kafka) to decouple the parser from backend services, enhancing scalability and reliability. --- ### 3. Implement Real-Time Log Monitoring Objective: Set up the Python script to monitor the Player.log file in real-time, triggering the log parser whenever new data is appended. #### 3.1. Using watchdog for File Monitoring The watchdog library in Python is a robust solution for monitoring file system events, such as modifications to files. In the provided parser.py script, watchdog is utilized to observe changes in Player.log and process new log entries as they occur. Key Components: - Observer: Monitors the file system for changes. - Event Handler ( LogHandler ): Defines actions to take when specific events (like file modifications) are detected. #### 3.2. Handling Log Rotation MTG Arena may rotate log files periodically, renaming old logs and creating new ones. To handle log rotation gracefully: 1. Monitor All Relevant Log Files: - Instead of watching a single Player.log , monitor the entire Logs directory and handle new log files as they are created. 2. Modify the Event Handler: Update the LogHandler to detect new files and adjust monitoring accordingly. # Enhanced LogHandler to handle log rotation class LogHandler(FileSystemEventHandler): def __init__(self, db): super().__init__() self.db = db self.file_handlers = {} self._initialize_existing_logs() def _initialize_existing_logs(self): # Initialize file handlers for existing Player.log files import glob log_files = glob.glob(LOG_FILE_PATH) # Adjust pattern if needed for file_path in log_files: self.add_file(file_path) def add_file(self, file_path): if file_path in self.file_handlers: return try: file = open(file_path, 'r', encoding='utf-8') file.seek(0, 2) # Move to end of file self.file_handlers[file_path] = file logger.info(f"Started monitoring {file_path}") except Exception as e: logger.error(f"Error opening file {file_path}: {e}") def remove_file(self, file_path): if file_path in self.file_handlers: self.file_handlers[file_path].close() del self.file_handlers[file_path] logger.info(f"Stopped monitoring {file_path}") def on_created(self, event): if 'Player.log' in event.src_path: self.add_file(event.src_path) def on_deleted(self, event): if 'Player.log' in event.src_path: self.remove_file(event.src_path) def on_modified(self, event): if event.src_path not in self.file_handlers: return file = self.file_handlers[event.src_path] for line in file: self.process_line(line.strip()) def process_line(self, line): # Same as before pass Explanation: - Initialization: On startup, the handler initializes monitoring for all existing Player.log files. - Dynamic Monitoring: Adds new log files when they are created and removes them when deleted, handling log rotation seamlessly. - Processing Lines: Reads new lines appended to any monitored Player.log file. Integration in main() : Ensure that the Observer is set to monitor the entire Logs directory to capture any new Player.log files created during log rotation. def main(): db = Database(DB_CONFIG) event_handler = LogHandler(db) observer = Observer() log_dir = re.sub(r'[^\\/]+$', '', LOG_FILE_PATH) # Get directory from log file path observer.schedule(event_handler, path=log_dir, recursive=False) observer.start() logger.info("Started log observer.") try: while True: time.sleep(1) except KeyboardInterrupt: observer.stop() logger.info("Stopping log observer.") except Exception as e: logger.error(f"Unexpected error: {e}") observer.stop() observer.join() db.close() --- ### 4. Store Parsed Data in PostgreSQL Objective: Modify the log parser to insert the extracted draft data into the PostgreSQL database, ensuring accurate mapping and maintaining proper relationships. #### 4.1. Ensuring Database Schema Alignment Before inserting data, verify that your database schema aligns with the data being parsed. Refer to the Database Schema section in 2.3 . #### 4.2. Enhancing the Parser for Data Integrity 1. Unique Constraints: - Ensure that drafts.id , drafted_cards.id , and other UUIDs are unique. - Utilize ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING or ON CONFLICT UPDATE to handle duplicate entries gracefully. 2. Foreign Key Constraints: - Enforce foreign key constraints to maintain referential integrity between tables (e.g., drafted_cards.draft_id references drafts.id ). 3. Data Validation: - Validate all incoming data to ensure that draftId , userId , and cardId are valid UUIDs and exist in their respective tables. 4. Transaction Management: - Use database transactions to ensure that multiple related insertions either all succeed or all fail, maintaining data consistency. Example Enhancement: def insert_drafted_card(self, draft_id, user_id, card_id, picked_at): if not all(is_valid_uuid(val) for val in [draft_id, user_id, card_id]): logger.warning(f"Invalid UUID in drafted_card: draft_id={draft_id}, user_id={user_id}, card_id={card_id}") return try: with self.conn.cursor() as cur: cur.execute( """ INSERT INTO drafted_cards (id, draft_id, user_id, card_id, picked_at) VALUES (gen_random_uuid(), %s, %s, %s, %s) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING; """, (draft_id, user_id, card_id, picked_at) ) logger.info(f"Inserted drafted card: draft_id={draft_id}, user_id={user_id}, card_id={card_id}") except Exception as e: logger.error(f"Error inserting drafted card: {e}") Note: Ensure that the gen_random_uuid() function is available in your PostgreSQL setup. If not, you can use uuid_generate_v4() provided by the uuid-ossp extension. #### 4.3. Handling Recommendations If you're storing recommendations, ensure that they are generated based on the current draft state. The parser inserts a dummy score, but in practice, you'd integrate with your recommendation engine. Example: # After inserting a drafted card, trigger recommendation generation def process_line(self, line): # Existing parsing logic... # After inserting drafted card # Trigger recommendation logic here generate_recommendations(draft_id) Recommendation Generation Function: def generate_recommendations(draft_id): # Fetch current draft state # Calculate synergy scores based on picks # Insert recommendations into the database pass Note: The actual implementation depends on your recommendation engine's design and requirements. --- ### 5. Update Backend Services with Parsed Data Objective: Ensure that backend services listen for new draft data in PostgreSQL and update real-time WebSocket clients with the latest information using Socket.IO. #### 5.1. Choosing the Communication Method There are multiple ways to notify the backend services about new data in PostgreSQL: 1. Polling: - Backend periodically queries the database for new data. - Cons: Inefficient and can lead to delays. 2. Database Triggers with Notification: - Use PostgreSQL's LISTEN / NOTIFY feature to send notifications to backend services when specific events occur. - Pros: Efficient and real-time. 3. Message Queues: - Utilize message brokers like RabbitMQ or Kafka to send messages from the parser to the backend. - Pros: Decoupled architecture, scalable. Recommended Approach: Use PostgreSQL's LISTEN / NOTIFY feature for simplicity and real-time communication. #### 5.2. Implementing PostgreSQL LISTEN/NOTIFY Step 1: Modify the Parser to Use NOTIFY After inserting data, send a NOTIFY event. def insert_drafted_card(self, draft_id, user_id, card_id, picked_at): if not all(is_valid_uuid(val) for val in [draft_id, user_id, card_id]): logger.warning(f"Invalid UUID in drafted_card: draft_id={draft_id}, user_id={user_id}, card_id={card_id}") return try: with self.conn.cursor() as cur: cur.execute( """ INSERT INTO drafted_cards (id, draft_id, user_id, card_id, picked_at) VALUES (gen_random_uuid(), %s, %s, %s, %s) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING; """, (draft_id, user_id, card_id, picked_at) ) logger.info(f"Inserted drafted card: draft_id={draft_id}, user_id={user_id}, card_id={card_id}") # Send NOTIFY payload = json.dumps({ 'draft_id': draft_id, 'user_id': user_id, 'card_id': card_id, 'picked_at': picked_at }) cur.execute("NOTIFY new_pick, %s;", (payload,)) except Exception as e: logger.error(f"Error inserting drafted card: {e}") Step 2: Set Up the Backend to Listen for Notifications In your Node.js backend ( index.js ), use the pg library to listen for NOTIFY events and emit Socket.IO events to clients. 1. Install pg Library: npm install pg 2. Modify index.js to Listen for Notifications: // index.js (continued) const { Pool } = require('pg'); const socketIo = require('socket.io'); // PostgreSQL connection pool for LISTEN/NOTIFY const pool = new Pool({ user: 'your_db_username', host: 'localhost', database: 'mtg_draft', password: 'your_db_password', port: 5432, }); pool.connect((err, client, release) => { if (err) { return logger.error('Error acquiring client', err.stack); } client.on('notification', (msg) => { if (msg.channel === 'new_pick') { const payload = JSON.parse(msg.payload); const { draft_id, user_id, card_id, picked_at } = payload; // Emit Socket.IO event to clients in the draft room io.to( draft_${draft_id} ).emit('newPick', { userId: user_id, cardId: card_id, pickedAt: picked_at, }); logger.info( Emitted newPick for draft ${draft_id} ); } }); client.query('LISTEN new_pick'); logger.info('Listening for new_pick notifications.'); }); // ... existing Socket.IO setup ... Explanation: - PostgreSQL Listener: - Connects to the PostgreSQL database using pg . - Listens for new_pick notifications. - Parses the JSON payload received from the NOTIFY event. - Emits a newPick event via Socket.IO to all clients in the corresponding draft room. Security Considerations: - Ensure that the PostgreSQL user used for LISTEN/NOTIFY has appropriate permissions. - Secure database credentials using environment variables or a secrets manager. #### 5.3. Enhancing Real-Time Updates 1. Broadcasting Additional Data: - Depending on your application's needs, you might want to emit more detailed information, such as the user's name or the card details. - Fetch additional data from the database if necessary before emitting. 2. Handling Socket.IO Client Events: - Ensure that your frontend clients are set up to handle the newPick event and update the UI accordingly. Example: Frontend Socket.IO Client Handling // client.js (Frontend) import { io } from "socket.io-client"; const socket = io('https://your-backend-domain.com', { auth: { token: 'your_jwt_token_here' } }); // Join a draft room after connecting socket.on('connect', () => { console.log( Connected with ID: ${socket.id} ); const draftId = '123e4567-e89b-12d3-a456-426614174000'; // Example draft ID socket.emit('joinDraft', draftId); }); // Handle newPick event socket.on('newPick', (data) => { console.log('New pick made:', data); // Update the UI with the new pick // e.g., add the picked card to the user's drafted cards }); // Handle errors socket.on('error', (error) => { console.error('Socket.IO Error:', error.message); }); Explanation: - newPick Event: - Receives data about the new draft pick. - Updates the frontend UI to reflect the new pick in real-time. --- ### 6. Comprehensive Example: Finalized parser.py and Backend Integration #### 6.1. Finalized parser.py Here's the complete parser.py incorporating all the discussed features: # parser.py import time import json import re import psycopg2 from watchdog.observers import Observer from watchdog.events import FileSystemEventHandler from config import LOG_FILE_PATH, DB_CONFIG import logging from uuid import UUID # Setup logging logging.basicConfig( level=logging.INFO, format='%(asctime)s [%(levelname)s] %(message)s', handlers=[ logging.FileHandler("parser.log"), logging.StreamHandler() ] ) logger = logging.getLogger(__name__) # Helper function to validate UUID def is_valid_uuid(val): try: UUID(str(val)) return True except ValueError: return False # Database connection handler class Database: def __init__(self, config): self.config = config self.conn = None self.connect() def connect(self): try: self.conn = psycopg2.connect(**self.config) self.conn.autocommit = True logger.info("Connected to PostgreSQL database.") except Exception as e: logger.error(f"Failed to connect to PostgreSQL: {e}") self.conn = None def reconnect(self): if self.conn: self.conn.close() self.connect() def insert_draft(self, draft_id): if not is_valid_uuid(draft_id): logger.warning(f"Attempted to insert invalid draft_id: {draft_id}") return try: with self.conn.cursor() as cur: cur.execute( """ INSERT INTO drafts (id, status) VALUES (%s, %s) ON CONFLICT (id) DO NOTHING; """, (draft_id, 'ongoing') ) logger.info(f"Inserted draft: {draft_id}") except Exception as e: logger.error(f"Error inserting draft {draft_id}: {e}") def insert_drafted_card(self, draft_id, user_id, card_id, picked_at): if not all(is_valid_uuid(val) for val in [draft_id, user_id, card_id]): logger.warning(f"Invalid UUID in drafted_card: draft_id={draft_id}, user_id={user_id}, card_id={card_id}") return try: with self.conn.cursor() as cur: cur.execute( """ INSERT INTO drafted_cards (id, draft_id, user_id, card_id, picked_at) VALUES (gen_random_uuid(), %s, %s, %s, %s) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING; """, (draft_id, user_id, card_id, picked_at) ) logger.info(f"Inserted drafted card: draft_id={draft_id}, user_id={user_id}, card_id={card_id}") # Send NOTIFY payload = json.dumps({ 'draft_id': draft_id, 'user_id': user_id, 'card_id': card_id, 'picked_at': picked_at }) cur.execute("NOTIFY new_pick, %s;", (payload,)) except Exception as e: logger.error(f"Error inserting drafted card: {e}") def insert_recommendation(self, draft_id, card_id, score): if not all(is_valid_uuid(val) for val in [draft_id, card_id]): logger.warning(f"Invalid UUID in recommendation: draft_id={draft_id}, card_id={card_id}") return try: with self.conn.cursor() as cur: cur.execute( """ INSERT INTO recommendations (id, draft_id, card_id, score) VALUES (gen_random_uuid(), %s, %s, %s) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING; """, (draft_id, card_id, score) ) logger.info(f"Inserted recommendation: draft_id={draft_id}, card_id={card_id}, score={score}") except Exception as e: logger.error(f"Error inserting recommendation: {e}") def close(self): if self.conn: self.conn.close() logger.info("Closed PostgreSQL connection.") # Log Event Handler class LogHandler(FileSystemEventHandler): def __init__(self, db): super().__init__() self.db = db self.file_handlers = {} self._initialize_existing_logs() def _initialize_existing_logs(self): # Initialize file handlers for existing Player.log files import glob log_files = glob.glob(LOG_FILE_PATH) # Adjust pattern if needed for file_path in log_files: self.add_file(file_path) def add_file(self, file_path): if file_path in self.file_handlers: return try: file = open(file_path, 'r', encoding='utf-8') file.seek(0, 2) # Move to end of file self.file_handlers[file_path] = file logger.info(f"Started monitoring {file_path}") except Exception as e: logger.error(f"Error opening file {file_path}: {e}") def remove_file(self, file_path): if file_path in self.file_handlers: self.file_handlers[file_path].close() del self.file_handlers[file_path] logger.info(f"Stopped monitoring {file_path}") def on_created(self, event): if 'Player.log' in event.src_path: self.add_file(event.src_path) def on_deleted(self, event): if 'Player.log' in event.src_path: self.remove_file(event.src_path) def on_modified(self, event): if event.src_path not in self.file_handlers: return file = self.file_handlers[event.src_path] for line in file: self.process_line(line.strip()) def process_line(self, line): # Example log line: # [2024-04-27 12:00:00.000] [Player] [INFO] DraftPick: {"draftId":"123e4567-e89b-12d3-a456-426614174000","userId":"user-uuid-1","cardId":"card-uuid-1","timestamp":"2024-04-27T12:00:00Z"} # Check if the line contains 'DraftPick' if 'DraftPick' not in line: return try: # Extract JSON part using regex json_part = re.search(r'DraftPick:\s*(\{.*\})', line) if not json_part: logger.warning(f"No JSON data found in line: {line}") return data = json.loads(json_part.group(1)) draft_id = data.get('draftId') user_id = data.get('userId') card_id = data.get('cardId') timestamp = data.get('timestamp') # ISO format if not all([draft_id, user_id, card_id, timestamp]): logger.warning(f"Incomplete data in line: {line}") return # Insert draft if not exists self.db.insert_draft(draft_id) # Insert drafted card self.db.insert_drafted_card(draft_id, user_id, card_id, timestamp) # (Optional) Generate and insert recommendations # This depends on your recommendation engine implementation # For demonstration, let's assume a dummy score score = 90 # Replace with actual scoring logic self.db.insert_recommendation(draft_id, card_id, score) # (Optional) Trigger backend update via API or message queue # Already handled via NOTIFY and backend listening except json.JSONDecodeError as e: logger.error(f"JSON decode error for line: {line} | Error: {e}") except Exception as e: logger.error(f"Error processing line: {line} | Error: {e}") # Main Function def main(): db = Database(DB_CONFIG) event_handler = LogHandler(db) observer = Observer() log_dir = re.sub(r'[^\\/]+$', '', LOG_FILE_PATH) # Get directory from log file path observer.schedule(event_handler, path=log_dir, recursive=False) observer.start() logger.info("Started log observer.") try: while True: time.sleep(1) except KeyboardInterrupt: observer.stop() logger.info("Stopping log observer.") except Exception as e: logger.error(f"Unexpected error: {e}") observer.stop() observer.join() db.close() if __name__ == "__main__": main() Key Features: - Log Rotation Handling: Monitors the entire Logs directory to handle the creation of new Player.log files seamlessly. - Database Notifications: Utilizes PostgreSQL's NOTIFY mechanism to inform backend services about new draft picks. - Error Handling: Comprehensive error logging ensures that issues are recorded and can be addressed promptly. #### 6.2. Exporting Socket.IO Instance To allow the parser.py to communicate with the backend, you need to export the Socket.IO instance from your Node.js backend. However, in the provided setup, the communication is handled via PostgreSQL's LISTEN/NOTIFY , making direct Socket.IO instance sharing unnecessary. Explanation: - The parser inserts data into PostgreSQL and sends a NOTIFY event. - The Node.js backend listens for NOTIFY events and emits Socket.IO events to frontend clients. - This decouples the parser from the backend, enhancing scalability and reliability. No Changes Needed in Backend for Socket.IO Instance Sharing. --- ### 7. Best Practices and Recommendations 1. Secure Credentials: - Avoid hardcoding sensitive information like database credentials in your scripts. - Use environment variables or a dedicated configuration management system. 2. Logging: - Maintain comprehensive logs to monitor parser activities and troubleshoot issues. - Implement log rotation for parser.log to prevent uncontrolled growth. 3. Error Handling: - Ensure that the parser can handle unexpected log formats and database errors without crashing. - Implement retry mechanisms for transient errors. 4. Performance Optimization: - For high-frequency log updates, consider optimizing database insertions (e.g., using batch inserts). - Monitor parser performance and adjust as needed. 5. Scalability: - If the application scales to multiple users or drafts, ensure that the parser and backend can handle increased load. - Consider distributing the parsing workload or implementing load balancing. 6. Testing: - Continuously test the parser with diverse log entries to ensure robustness. - Implement automated tests to validate parser functionality. 7. Documentation: - Document the parser's functionality, configuration options, and deployment instructions. - Maintain clear documentation for backend integrations and API endpoints. 8. Deployment: - Run the parser as a background service or daemon to ensure continuous operation. - Use tools like systemd (Linux) or pm2 (Node.js) for process management. Example: Running the Parser with pm2 1. Install pm2 : npm install -g pm2 2. Start the Parser: pm2 start parser.py --name mtg-log-parser 3. Monitor Parser Logs: pm2 logs mtg-log-parser 4. Ensure Parser Starts on System Boot: pm2 startup pm2 save --- ### 9.
